preparation: Administer IM undiluted in the thigh
administration: Repeat IM injection every 5 minutes if needed x 2-3 doses then switch to the IV infusion if symptoms persist. If patient is unconscious and requiring assisted ventilation secondary to anaphylaxis, favor IV epinephrine - push dose epinephrine (See EZResus Pediatric).  Dose of 0.1 mg can be used for weights of 3-9 Kg if 1 mL syringe not available. Dose of 0.15 mg acceptable for patient <15 Kg if auto-injector is the only epinephrine available.
particularity: Avoid 1:1000 nomenclature as it is associated with more medication errors.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted
administration: Administer IV q3-5min PRN in cardiac arrest  Administer the 1st dose within the first 5 minutes of resuscitation then at every 2 rhythm analysis/pulse checks (q4min) to facilitate management.
particularity: Avoid 1:10,000 nomenclature as it is associated with more medication errors.

preparation: Administer IV DILUTED
administration: Presentation: Pre-filled Syringe of Epinephrine 0.1 mg/mL (100 mcg/mL) 1) Withdraw 1 mL of Epinephrine 100 mcg/mL with a 10 mL syringe 2) Add 9 mL of NaCl 0.9% to the syringe 3) Final concentration = 100 mcg dans 10 mL = 10 mcg/mL 4) Draw the required dose using a syringe 5) Clearly identify syringes with the correct concentration 6) Administer IV push q1-5min PRN to a target blood pressure or heart rate
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.   Although the preferred route of administration of vasopressors is via central venous access, multiple studies demonstrate that administration via a good quality peripheral access is safe if used for a short period of time and with frequent verifications of the access site.

route: Nebulization every 20 min as needed x 3 doses and reassess
preparation: Administer by nebulizer diluted up to 3-5 mL of NaCl 0,9%
administration: • Administer if stridor • Do not use routinely in case of bronchiolitis or acute severe asthma
particularity: Nil

administration: Caution: Several concentrations at 16, 20 and 40 mcg/mL pending standardization according to ASHP standards.  Use if IV salbutamol is not available or if the patient remains symptomatic despite high dose of IV salbutamol.   Suggested titration: 1) Start infusion at 0.02-0.1 mcg/kg/min. 2) Titrate by increments of 0.01-0.02 mcg/kg/min q5min depending on clinical response. (Expert Opinion)  Additional information: Do NOT remove volume during preparation. • Rinse the tubing with the infusion before administration. • Use the lowest concentration if a peripheral venous access is used.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR, MgSO4. Avoid 1:1000 nomenclature as it is associated with more medication errors.  Although the preferred route of administration of vasopressors is via central venous access, multiple studies demonstrate that administration via a good quality peripheral access is safe if used for a short period of time and with frequent verifications of the access site.

administration: Caution: Several concentrations at 16, 20 and 40 mcg/mL pending standardization according to ASHP standards.  Suggested titration: 1) Start infusion at 0.05 mcg/kg/min 2) Titrate by increments of 0.01-0.02 mcg/kg/min q5min depending on clinical response. (Expert Opinion)  Additional information: • Do NOT remove volume during preparation. • Rinse the tubing with the infusion before administration. • In cardiogenic shock, the reported doses are 0.02-0.1 mcg/Kg/min. In distributive septic shock, the reported doses are 0.05-1 mcg/Kg/min. In practice, the addition of the vasoconstrictor effect (alpha) to the inotropic effect (beta) is not precise and absolute. • Use the lowest concentration if a peripheral venous access is used.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  Avoid 1:1000 nomenclature as it is associated with more medication errors.  Although the preferred route of administration of vasopressors is via central venous access, multiple studies demonstrate that administration via a good quality peripheral access is safe if used for a short period of time and with frequent verifications of the access site.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0,9%
administration: IV over 3-5 minutes to minimize adverse effects.
particularity: Wait 3 minutes for optimal analgesic effect. Protect ampoule from light.

administration: Presentation : - MAD-300 device - Luer-Lock syringe 1 or 3 mL - Fentanyl 50 mcg/mL  1) Nasal irrigation before administration as needed 2) Draw the desired dose and add 0.1 mL to fill the dead space 3) Connect the MAD-300 device to the syringe 4) Divide the dose evenly between the 2 nostrils (possible volume of 0.2-1 mL per nostril) 5) Administer in 1-2 seconds  Not recommended for use in patients less than 6 months
particularity: Use the 50 mcg/mL solution for injection.  Dose may be completed to a total of 2 mcg/Kg or 100 mcg. Wait 5 min for optimal analgesic effect. Protect ampoules from light.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted
administration: Caution:  Increased risk of chest wall rigidity in neonates linked to the rate of administration and high doses. In the context of intubation, the use of a neuromuscular blocker treats chest wall rigidity.  Additional information: • Dose 3-5 mcg/kg if used as the ONLY induction agent in rapid sequence intubation  • Fentanyl is not a first choice as a sole induction agent EXCEPT in neonatology.
particularity: Nil

preparation: Administer IV undiluted
administration: In neonatal patients or < 3 kg, favor succinylcholine as a neuromuscular blocker in the absence of contraindications.  Absolute contraindications • History of malignant hyperthermia • History of pseudocholinesterase deficiency • Significant tissue damage (Rhabdomyolysis, Severe burn > 24-48h, Abdominal sepsis >5 days) • Neuromuscular disease • Hyperkalemia > 5.5 mmol/L • Renal failure • Stroke / Spinal cord injury > 5 days - 6 months
particularity: Keep refrigerated. Stable at room temperature for 6 months.  With dose of 1.5-2 mg/Kg: Onset of action: 15 seconds Duration of action: 4-6 minutes

preparation: Administer IV undiluted
administration: Slow injection may cause paradoxical bradycardia. The minimum dose of 0.1 mg is no longer recommended.  PALS 2020 recommends atropine for the treatment of bradycardia with hemodynamic instability secondary to vagal stimulation, primary AV block excluding high-grade AV block, or intoxication. There is no evidence that atropine treats bradycardia from other causes.
particularity: If high risk of bradycardia secondary to catecholamine depletion (ex.: patient in shock), treat with vasopressor agents (Epinephrine as a push dose pressor or continuous drip) rather than with atropine.  If patient requiring repeated neurological examination (ex.: head trauma patient), treat with vasopressor agents (epinephrine as a rapid bolus vasopressor or continuous infusion) rather than with atropine. Atropine causes loss of pupillary assessment (mydriasis) lasting 30-45 minutes.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted
administration: • Double the dose every 5 minutes until muscarinic signs resolve (bronchorrhea, bradycardia, hypotension). In the presence of muscarinic symptoms, administer atropine even if the patient is tachycardic. • Consider starting an atropine infusion.  • Slow injection may cause paradoxical bradycardia. • The minimum dose of 0.1 mg is no longer recommended.
particularity: There is no maximum cumulative dose of atropine in the treatment of organophosphate toxicity. Tachycardia is not a contraindication to administration.

administration: Calculate the infusion dose based on the cumulative bolus dose 1) Calculate the dose corresponding to 10 to 20% of the effective cumulative bolus dose (max initial dose 3 mg/h) 2)  Start the infusion to administer this calculated dose over 1 hour 3) Adjust perfusion based on signs of under- or over-atropinization 4) Atropinization is defined as: resolution of bronchorrhea + bradycardia + hypotension + dry axillae   Example: The patient is atropinized after 3 boluses: 1st bolus 0.5 mg, 2nd bolus 1 mg, 3rd bolus 2 mg 1) Cumulative effective bolus dose = 1+2+3 = 6 mg 2) 10 to 20% of this dose = 0.6-1.2 mg 3) Start the infusion at 0.6-1.2 mg/h   Caution: multiple concentrations available. Dilute dose in NaCl 0.9% to achieve a final concentration of 0.1 mg/mL, administer using a volumetric pump.   DILUTED 0.1 mg/mL solution:  Presentation: Syringe of Atropine 0.1 mg/mL (5 mL or 10 mL). 1) Use an empty 50 mL or 100 mL bag. 2) Add 50 mL (5 mg) of Atropine 0.1 mg/mL to the bag. 3) Final concentration = 0.1 mg/mL 4) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting administration   Presentation: Syringe of Atropine 0.2 mg/mL (1 mg in 5 mL). 1) Withdraw 25 mL from a 50 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% = 25 mL 2) Add 25 mL (5 mg) of Atropine 0.2 mg/mL to the bag 3) Final concentration = 5 mg in 50 mL = 0.1 mg/mL 4) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting administration  Presentation: Vial of Atropine 0.4 mg/mL (0.4 mg in 1 mL). 1) Withdraw 12.5 mL from a 50 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% = 37.5 mL 2) Add 12.5 mL (5 mg) of Atropine 0.4 mg/mL to the bag 3) Final concentration = 5 mg in 50 mL = 0.1 mg/mL 4) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting administration   Presentation: Vial of Atropine 0.6 mg/mL (0.6 mg in 1 mL). 1) Withdraw 8.3 mL from a 50 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% = 41.7 mL 2) Add 8.3 mL (5 mg) of Atropine 0.6 mg/mL to the bag 3) Final concentration = 5 mg in 50 mL = 0.1 mg/mL 4) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting administration
particularity: There is no maximum cumulative dose of atropine in the treatment of organophosphate toxicity. Tachycardia is not a contraindication to administration.

administration: Avoid in patients with suspected metabolic disorders, hepatic insufficiency, coagulopathy or women who are pregnant or of childbearing age. Warning, drug interactions involving multiple CYP 450.  No data to justify dilution in less than 50 mL and concentrated administration via syringe pump.
particularity: Drug available through Health Canada's Special Access Program; not commercially available in Canada.  Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  Maximum speed of administration of 6 mg/kg/min.  Subsequent doses of 5-30 mg/Kg (max adult 1500 mg) IV q12h should be considered depending on the clinical outcome. These doses can also be administered orally for the bioavailability close to 100%. Adjust dosage according to serum levels.

administration: Use a good caliber proximal venous access such as right upper extremity elbow crease. Administration via distal peripheral route (eg: access on the foot) decreases the chances of success and may require higher doses (eg: 0.3 mg/Kg).  Ideally administer while recording a 12-lead ECG. Defibrillator and resuscitation cart nearby.  Double-syringe or 3-way stopcock administration technique:  Preparation: 1) Withdraw the dose of adenosine, with an additional 0.3 mL, with a 1, 3 or 5 mL syringe 2) Open the valve to inlet 1 (inlet 2 blocked, patient outlet open) 3) Screw the adenosine syringe onto inlet 1 and purge the tap with 0.3 mL of adenosine 4) Adjust the volume of adenosine to the desired dose in the syringe 5) Block entry 1 (adenosine) 6) Screw a 10 mL syringe of NaCl 0.9% onto inlet 2 7) Purge the 3-way stopcock with approximately 2 mL of NaCl 0.9%  Administration: 1) Connect the output of the device to the patient 2) Open the valve to inlet 1 (adenosine) and administer the adenosine in 1-3 seconds 3) Open the valve to inlet 2 (NaCl 0.9%) and rinse QUICKLY with NaCl 0.9% (< 5 Kg = 3 mL; >5 Kg = 10 mL)  Single-syringe diluted with NaCl 0.9% technique : For patient > 8 years old with venous access of good caliber  (Must be able to support the administration of 20 mL in 1-3 seconds) (Expert opinion)  1) Dilute the dose of adenosine with NaCl 0.9% for a total volume of 20 mL 2) Administer with a single IV syringe into the port closest to the patient, within 1-3 seconds.
particularity: Consider reducing the dose by 50% if:  - Administrated via central venous access - Transplanted heart - Concomitant carbamazepine use

preparation: Administer IV DILUTED
administration: DILUTED 1.74 mg/mL solution  Further dilution for administration via pump: Presentation: Vial of Amiodarone 50 mg/mL (150 mg in 3 mL). 1) Use a 250 mL bag of D5% WITHOUT DEHP 2) Add 9 mL (3 vials) of Amiodarone 50 mg/mL to the bag, WITHOUT removing volume 3) Final concentration = 450 mg in 259 mL = 1.74 mg/mL 4) Install a 0.22 micron filter at the end of the tubing 5) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting the administration 6) For a dose of 5 mg/Kg, administer 2.9 mL/Kg of the solution diluted to 1,74 mg/mL over 60 min.  Additional information: • Except in cardiac arrest, the administration of amiodarone over < 20 min increases the risk of severe hypotension and hepatotoxicity which can be fatal. • Can be administered over 30 min in patients > 30 kg. • The use of bags without DEHP is recommended to reduce the release of plasticizers. For this reason, administration via syringe pump is not advised, as syringes are typically made of PVC. • Solution diluted to 6 mg/mL is possible, but requires a central venous access.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  Repeatable bolus up to a maximum of 15 mg/Kg (3 boluses) Do not use in case of doubt for Torsades de Pointes and assess risks/benefits with cardiology if QTc> 500 msec.

administration: Caution:  • Use 250 mL bag of D5% WITHOUT DEHP for preparation of the infusion. • Administer with a 0.22 micron filter  Further dilution for administration via pump: 1) Prepare a DILUTED 1.74 mg/mL solution 2) Do NOT remove volume during preparation 3) Install a 0.22 micron filter at the end of the tubing 4) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting the administration 5) After the 5 mg/Kg bolus, start the continuous infusion at 5-15 mcg/Kg/min.  Additional information: • The use of bags without DEHP is recommended to reduce the release of plasticizers. For this reason, administration via syringe pump is not advised, as syringes are typically made of PVC. • If central venous access not available, can be administered via proximal peripheral venous access of good caliber. • Solution diluted to 6 mg/mL are possible, but requires a central venous access.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  Repeatable bolus up to a maximum of 15 mg/Kg (3 boluses) Do not use in case of doubt for Torsades de Pointes and assess risks/benefits with cardiology if QTc> 500 msec.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0,9%
administration: Repeat every 60 seconds as needed for 5 doses total (maximum cumulative dose: 1 mg or 0.05 mg/kg).  Relapses in sedation may occur approximately 90 minutes after the first administration. Repeat bolus or start IV infusion as needed.  Contraindications: - Coma of unknown origin - Co-ingestion of another toxic substance - Epilepsy - Benzodiazepine dependence - ECG compatible with sodium channel blocker intoxication - Intubated patient
particularity: Nil

administration: Repeat every 60 seconds as needed for 5 doses total (maximum cumulative dose: 1 mg or 0.05 mg/kg). Relapses in sedation may occur approximately 90 minutes after the first administration. Repeat bolus or start IV infusion as needed.  Contraindications: - Coma of unknown origin - Co-ingestion of another toxic substance - Epilepsy - Benzodiazepine dependence - ECG compatible with sodium channel blocker intoxication - Intubated patient    Further dilution for administration via pump: Presentation: Vial of Flumazenil 0,1 mg/mL (0.5 mg in 5 mL or 1 mg in 10 mL) 1) Remove 20 mL from a 100 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% = 80 mL 2) Add 20 mL (2 mg = 4 vials) of Flumazenil 0.1 mg/mL to the bag 3) Final concentration = 2 mg in 100 mL = 0.02 mg/mL 4) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting the administration  5) Administer preferably through a proximal peripheral IV access to minimize pain during injection
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5%, NaCl, LR.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0,9%
administration: Additional information: • Usual dose for patient > 3 months of life: 1-1.5 mg/Kg • Repeat dose of 0.5mg/kg q5 minutes as needed. • Ketamine is not recommended for emergency procedural sedation < 3 months of life due to increased risk of laryngospasm, apnea and airway obstruction. Consider maximizing non-pharmacological measures and local therapies and if the need for IV procedural sedation persists, consider ketamine 0.5 mg/Kg under closer monitoring.
particularity: Laryngospasm risk <1%.

administration: • Use 50 mg/mL concentration • Not recommended for use in patients less than 6 months • For doses > 100 mg, administer in 2 sequences spaced 1-2 minutes apart. • Dose of 5-6 mg/Kg for use as monotherapy. Reduce dose if drug combination. • The combination with a benzodiazepine would not reduce the psychomimetic and emergence effects of ketamine.  Material : - MAD-300 device - 1 or 3 mL Luer-Lock syringe  1) Nasal irrigation before administration as needed 2) Draw the desired dose and add 0.1 mL to fill the dead space 3) Connect the MAD-300 device to the syringe 4) Divide the dose evenly between the 2 nostrils (possible volume of 0.2-1 mL per nostril) 5) Administer in 1-2 seconds
particularity: Wait 5 minutes for optimal sedative effect. Laryngospasm risk <1%.

indication: TET spells in Tetralogy of Fallot
preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0.9%
administration: Use in agitated or painful patient with hypoxic Tetralogy of Fallot.  Decreased heart and respiratory rate as well as systemic vasoconstriction due to ketamine reduces the right-to-left shunt and improves pulmonary blood flow in the tetralogy of Fallot.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl 0.9%, LR, KCl

preparation: Administer IM undiluted in the thigh
administration: • Use 50 mg/mL concentration • Reserved for patients who are dangerous to themselves or those around them • For 30 Kg and more, do not exceed 5 mL per injection site • For less than 30 Kg, do not exceed 3 mL per injection site • Use two injection sites if needed
particularity: • Close monitoring of hemodynamic and respiratory status, especially if administration of other molecules before ketamine. • Consider using IV or IM benzodiazepines as a first choice, depending on the clinical situation.
reference_snippet: - Barbic D, Andolfatto G, Grunau B, Scheuermeyer FX, Macewan B, Qian H, Wong H, Barbic SP, Honer WG. Rapid Agitation Control With Ketamine in the Emergency Department: A Blinded, Randomized Controlled Trial. Ann Emerg Med. 2021 Aug 2:S0196-0644(21)00433-9. doi: 10.1016/j.annemergmed.2021.05.023. Epu

particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl 0.9%, LR, KCl.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0,9%
administration: • Preferentially use of the 10 mg/mL concentration • Ketamine is usually not a first choice for post-intubation sedation and analgesia, but is an alternative in hemodynamically unstable or refractory patients. • If the patient is hemodynamically unstable or has severe heart failure, administer the bolus over at least 5 minutes. • According to recent literature, ketamine is not contraindicated in intracranial hypertension and reduces ICP • Can increase bispectral index (BIS)
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl 0.9%, LR, KCl and MgSO4.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0,9%
administration: Asthma in patients < 9 months of life is very rare.  Suggested titration: 1) Bolus of 0.5-2 mg/Kg 2) Start the infusion at 0.5 mg/Kg/h 3) Additional bolus of 0.5-2 mg/Kg as needed, between each increase in the infusion 4) Increase the infusion by 0.5 mg/Kg/h q15-30min depending on respiratory and neurological status. (Expert opinion)  Additional information: • Close monitoring of the level of consciousness and respiratory drive if the patient is not intubated. • Have equipment and experimented personnel ready for airway management.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl 0.9%, LR, KCl

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0,9%
administration: • Combine the ketamine infusion with another infusion agent. (ex .: midazolam) • Ketamine is not used in neonatal refractory status epilepticus; consider phenobarbital coma on the recommendation of neonatology and pediatric neurology if patient < 4 weeks of life.  Suggested titration: 1) Bolus of 1-3 mg/Kg 2) Start the infusion at 1 mg/Kg/h 3) Additional bolus of 1-3 mg/Kg between each increase in the infusion 4) Increase the infusion by 1 mg/Kg/h q15-30min until the convulsions stop. (Expert opinion)
particularity: • Under EEG monitoring • Have equipment and personnel ready for airway management • Incompatible with LR. Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl • The early use in Refractory Status Epilepticus of a lower dose ketamine infusion (0.1-1 mg/Kg/h) could also be effective, but doses between 1-10 mg/Kg/h are more documented. (Expert opinion)

preparation: Administer IV undiluted
administration: • Ensure adequate analgo-sedation is in place before administration to prevent a paralyzed patient not receiving analgesia and sedation.  • The rocuronium dose of 1.5 mg/Kg has not been studied in pediatrics; if the patient is not adequately paralyzed after a first dose, repeat the dose after 90 seconds.  • If repeated doses or rocuronium, monitor the effect of rocuronium with Train-of-four and the level of analgosedation with a bispectral index (BIS) or other type of electroencephalographic monitoring if available. • For emergency reversal of rocuronium, use Sugammadex 16 mg/Kg • Provide passive eyelid closure and eye lubrication for prevention corneal abrasions
particularity: Keep refrigerated. Stable at room temperature for 6 months.  With dose of 1-1.2 mg/Kg: Onset of action: 45-60 seconds Duration of action: 30-45 minutes

administration: Not recommended as a first line agent in ALL types of pediatric shock. Prefer either epinephrine or norepinephrine in case of septic shock and epinephrine in case of cardiogenic shock.  Suggested titration: 1) Start infusion at 5 mcg/kg/min. 2) Titrate by increments of 2.5-5 mcg/kg/min q5min until target BP is reached or a maximum dose of 20 mcg/Kg/min. (Expert Opinion)  Additional information: • Rinse the tubing with the infusion before administration • Use the lowest concentration if a peripheral venous access is used
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  Starting dose 5 mcg/Kg/min ("renal" dose of 2-3 mcg/Kg/min not effective according to the literature).  Although the preferred route of administration of vasopressors is via central venous access, multiple studies demonstrate that administration via a good quality peripheral access is safe if used for a short period of time and with frequent verifications of the access site.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted
administration: Risk of hypotension. Administer the commercial IV 10 mg/mL solution directly without rediluting. Protect from light.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  Relative contraindication in severe active liver disease or severe liver failure. Usual dosage: 15 mg/Kg/dose, max 1000 mg/dose IV q6h excluding patients <1 month and premature and patients with decreased renal function.  Avir Pharma's Canadian formulation contains povidone, an inactive compound removed by glomerular filtration. Although the potential for accumulation of povidone is unknown, there is a possibility in patients < 1 month of age (including premature infants), as well as in patients with severe renal impairment, where glomerular filtration is reduced. The consequences of this accumulation remain poorly defined for these populations. For repeat doses, carefully weigh the risks vs benefits.

indication: Acetaminophen intoxication, Quebec poison control protocol
administration: This is the protocol of the Quebec Poison Control Center  Administer 150 mg/Kg over 1 hour (first hour) then 15 mg/Kg/h for at least 20 hours or as recommended by the poison control center (see "infusion" step).  Usual dilution with D5W, but also compatible with NaCl 0.9% and NaCl 0.45%. Do NOT remove volume during preparation.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl. No compatibility data with LR.  To be started and stopped according to the criteria of your poison control center. In exceptional cases, the poison control center may recommend doubling the infusion. It is then necessary to double the concentration of the solute, and not to double the flow rate of the infusion.  Watch for the risk of an anaphylactoid reaction.

particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl. No compatibility data with LR.  To be started and stopped according to the criteria of your poison control center. In exceptional cases, the poison control center may recommend doubling the infusion. It is then necessary to double the concentration of the solute, and not to double the flow rate of the infusion.  Watch for the risk of an anaphylactoid reaction.

Manuel sur la pharmacothérapie parentérale de l’Hôpital d’Ottawa. 4e ed. Ottawa (Canada): Hôpital d'Ottawa; 2019. - American College of Medical Toxico

administration: • For patients with severe methemoglobinemia with a contraindication to methylene blue (e.g. suspected or documented G6PD deficiency), discuss with your local poison control center for extracorporal therapy. Consider ascorbic acid for mild cases (methemoglobinemia < 15%) that don't need rapidly acting therapy. • Usual interval: every 6 hours for up to 4 days or until methemoglobin levels normalize.  If 25 mL bag not available: 1) Remove 25 mL from a 50 mL bag 2) Add the indicated dose  Consider that the use of a bag of 25-50 mL can represent a significant amount of volume depending on the clinical situation.
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR. • The doses studied vary widely in the literature. (5-300 mg/Kg/day) • Can be administered IM undiluted. • Protect from light.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted with syringe pump
administration: Administer undiluted with syringe pump at a rate of 5 mg/min. If a syringe pump is not available, administer on pump at the same rate by putting in an empty bag.  In case of elevated anion gap without metabolic acidosis: 1 mg/kg. Repeat the dose every 6 hours until the intoxication resolves.  In case of elevated anion gap with metabolic acidosis: 2 mg/kg. Repeat the dose every 4-6 hours until the intoxication resolves and the metabolic acidosis is corrected. Adjust the dose and the interval according to the severity of the acidosis and patient characteristics (denutrition, alcoholism, etc.).
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.   Maximum doses: 1 mg/kg up to 50 mg and 2 mg/kg up to 100 mg. (Expert advice)
reference_snippet: - Canadian antidote guide in acute care toxicology [https://www.ciusss-capitalenationale.gouv.qc.ca/antidotes] - Howland MA, Folates: Leucovorin (Folinic Acid) and Folic Acid, In: Hoffman RS, Nelson LS, Howland MA, Lewin NA, Flomenbaum NE. Goldfrank’s manual of toxicologic emergencies, 10th ed: McGr

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 10 mL of NaCl 0,9%
administration: Do not administer via the same IV access as blood products.
particularity: • Consider for patients with post-thrombolytic hemorrhage, perioperative bleeding, dental treatments in a patients with coagulopathy, hemoptysis or uterine bleeding, but also for patients with traumatic bleeding (see separate entries for trauma). • Consider topical administration when possible (e.g. epistaxis, dental treatments). • Consider nebulization in patients with hemoptysis (see separate entry). • Tranexamic acid is not recommended for clinically significant upper gastrointestinal bleeding.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 10 mL of NaCl 0,9%
administration: Administration in 5 minutes is safe and keeps IV access available in case of emergency.
particularity: • Therapies for angioedema should never delay securing the airway. • The efficacy of tranexamic acid remains controversial in angioedema because of the lack of definitive studies. However, tranexamic acid is safe, widely available and easy to administer which makes it a therapy to consider for bradykinin-mediated angioedema. • For hereditary angioedema cases, administer 1st line agents when possible (e.g. C1-esterase inhibitor, icatibant).

preparation: Administer by nebulizer undiluted
administration: • Do not dilute for faster administration. • Consider using a mouth piece rather than a mask to better direct the nebulization in non intubated patients. • Can be instilled directly into the endotracheal tube followed by 1-2 mL (< 10 Kg) or 2-5 mL (≥ 10 Kg) of NaCl 0.9% in emergency situations in already intubated patients.  • In intubated infants, aspiration of clots post-hemoptysis may be difficult and require additional manipulation secondary to the small size of the endotracheal tube. Consider consulting a pediatric airway specialist promptly.
particularity: Can be repeated every 6-8 hours as needed.

indication: Hemorrhage associated with trauma (single bolus without drip)
preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 10 mL of NaCl 0,9%
administration: • Do not administer via the same IV access as blood products.  • Administration rate faster than 100 mg/min is commonly used in practice in trauma and other indications. (Expert opinion) • According to some studies and pediatric protocols from hospital centers specialized in traumatology, a bolus of 30 mg/Kg or 2 g alone (without infusion) could be equivalent to the standard method of administration (bolus + infusion).
particularity: • Should be given within three hours of trauma, otherwise less effective and possibly associated with adverse effects. • Benefits not demonstrated in isolated severe head trauma

particularity: Should be given within 3 hours of trauma otherwise less effective and possibly associated with adverse effects.

indication: HSV/VZV contact in infant with fever of unknown origin (< 6 weeks)
administration: Empiric coverage of HSV/VZV, without lesions, in the child > 6 weeks post-contact is not necessary unless there is clinical suspicion of viral encephalitis.  If 25 mL bag not available 1) Remove 25 mL from a 50 mL bag 2) Add the indicated dose 3) Administer at the indicated rate  Consider that the use of a bag of 25-50 mL can represent a significant amount of volume depending on the clinical situation.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.   Maintain adequate hydration during administration; dehydration increases the risk of crystallization in the renal tubules.  The first dose of an anti-infective medication (mg) should not be influenced by the presence or absence of renal insufficiency in order to achieve an effective concentration quickly. If adjustments are necessary, these should be applied at subsequent doses.  Usual dosing: 60 mg/Kg/day divided q8h excluding premature babies and patients with reduced renal function. Dosage adjustment in renal failure.

indication: Viral encephalitis (≥ 1 year, dose in m2)
administration: For patients ≥ 12 years old, Acyclovir 15 mg/Kg/dose = 500 mg/m2/dose.  Consider that the use of a bag of 50-100-250 mL can represent a significant amount of volume depending on the clinical situation.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.   Maintain adequate hydration during administration; dehydration increases the risk of crystallization in the kidney tubules.  The first dose of an anti-infective medication (mg) should not be influenced by the presence or absence of renal insufficiency in order to achieve an effective concentration quickly. If adjustments are necessary, these should be applied at subsequent doses.  Usual dosing: 1500 mg/m2/day divided q8h for patients > 1 year excluding patients with decreased renal function. Dosage adjustment in renal failure. Body surface area in m2, without measuring height, obtained by Costeff's formula: m2= [(4 x Kg)+ 7]/(90+ Kg).

administration: Suggested titration: 1) Start the infusion at 0.03-0.05 mcg/Kg/min. 2) Titrate by increments of 0.03-0.05 mcg/Kg/min according to the O2 saturation and/or arterial pressure objectives discussed with the experts, according to the suspected cardiopathy, to open or maintain the ductus arteriosus. 3) Once the ductus arteriosus is open and the patient is hemodynamically stabilized, titrate to obtain the minimum effective dose and minimize side effects. (e.g.: Decrease to 0.05 mcg/Kg/min then 0.02 mcg/Kg/min q6h ad 0.01 mcg/Kg/min according to echocardiography) (Expert opinion)  Do NOT remove volume during preparation. Rinse the tubing with the infusion before administration.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  If patient with reduced dextrose intake, very at risk of hypoglycaemia or with a history of hypoglycaemia, dilute in D10% rather than D5%. Keep refrigerated.  Monitor for hypotension, apnea and fever.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted directly from the vial after reconstitution
administration: Not indicated in children < 2 years old or if onset of symptoms not defined or > 4.5 hours. Validate the absence of contraindications with the neurologist before stroke thrombolysis.  Pediatric stroke requires radiological confirmation, either by CT or CT-angiography (normal parenchyma or with minimal ischemic change AND partial or complete occlusion of the corresponding artery) OR by MRI (acute stroke on diffusion sequences) combined with angio-MRI (partial or complete occlusion of the corresponding artery)  Reconstitution: Presentation: vial of Alteplase 100 mg, 100 mL vial of sterile water for injection  and transfer device Refer to the monograph for detailed reconstitution 1) Mix the alteplase vial with the diluent with the help of the transfer device provided. DO NOT shake the bottle. 2) Mix the solution with a gentle swirl to make sure the product is completely dissolved. 3) Visually inspect the solution for particulate matter and discoloration prior to administration. 4) Concentration after reconstitution = 1 mg/mL  Presentation: vial of Alteplase 50 mg Refer to the monograph for detailed reconstitution 1) Using a 50 mL syringe and the large needle (18G), take 50 mL of sterile water for injection.  2) Inject gently the sterile water into the alteplase vial. 3) Mix the solution with a gentle swirl to make sure the product is completely dissolved. 4) Visually inspect the solution for particulate matter and discoloration prior to administration. 5) Concentration after reconstitution = 1 mg/mL
concentration_note: Administer 10% of the total dose IV push
particularity: Incompatible in Y with D5W, LR or any other solutions. Only compatible with NaCl 0.9%.  Contraindications (Non-exhaustive list, see monograph or article by Fugate et al.)  Patient-related: - Age < 2 years - Systolic BP >15% of the 99th percentile or > 185 mmHg despite treatment - Current use of anticoagulants (heparin (< 48 hrs with abnormal aPTT), enoxaparin (< 24 hrs), dalteparin (< 24 hrs), warfarin, dabigatran, rivaroxaban, apixaban, edoxaban) - INR > 1.7, platelets <100 x 10^9/L, aPTT > 40 sec, PT > 15 sec, fibrinogen < 1 g/L despite treatment - Active coagulation disorder (e.g. Von Willebrand disease) - Glycemia < 4 mmol/L or > 22 mmol/L despite treatment - Neoplasia (< 1 month after completion of treatment) - Alteplase anaphylaxis  Presentation related: - Onset of symptoms not defined or > 4.5 hours - Presentation suggestive of subarachnoid hemorrhage (even if CT-scan without bleeding) - Stroke related to infectious endocarditis, meningitis, moyamoya, sickle cell anemia, fat or gas embolism - Presentation suggestive of myocardial infarction or post-infarction pericarditis - Active internal bleeding  Related to imagery - Hypodensity > 1/3 cerebral hemisphere on CT-scan - Intracranial bleeding - Intracranial dissection (defined as involvement at or distal to the ophthalmic artery) - Arterial or venous intracerebral malformation, aneurysm or cerebral neoplasia  History-related: - History of vasculitis or vasculitis of the primary or secondary central nervous system - In the last 7 days: arterial puncture on a non-compressible site - In the last 14 days: childbirth, major surgery or biopsy of parenchyma - In the last 21 days: digestive or urinary bleeding - In the last 3 months: intracranial or intraspinal surgery, severe head trauma, intracranial hemorrhage, ischemic stroke

preparation: Administer IV undiluted directly from the vial after reconstitution
administration: • There is no consensus on the dose of alteplase in pediatric massive pulmonary embolism. Doses vary from 0.1-0.6 mg/Kg/h over 6 hours for systemic thrombolysis with alteplase. The selected dose of 1.2 mg/Kg over 2 hours is the result of a consensus of local experts including intensivists and pediatric hematologists. Consider starting at a reduced dose in infants. For patients who remain symptomatic and hemodynamically unstable after 2 hrs of treatment, consider repeating another 2-hour course. • Consider administering a bolus of 0,12-0,25 mg/Kg, max 10 mg (10-20% of the total dose) IV push if the patient is very unstable. The rest of the dose can then be administered IV over 2 hours. • Withhold heparin before beginning alteplase administration. Heparin can be restarted when the aPTT < 40 sec (1.5 times the normal value)  Reconstitution:  Presentation: vial of Alteplase 100 mg, 100 mL vial of sterile water for injection  and transfer device Refer to the monograph for detailed reconstitution 1) Mix the alteplase vial with the diluent with the help of the transfer device provided. DO NOT shake the bottle. 2) Mix the solution with a gentle swirl to make sure the product is completely dissolved. 3) Visually inspect the solution for particulate matter and discoloration prior to administration. 4) Concentration after reconstitution = 1 mg/mL  Presentation: vial of Alteplase 50 mg Refer to the monograph for detailed reconstitution 1) Using a 50 mL syringe and the large needle (18G), take 50 mL of sterile water for injection.  2) Inject gently the sterile water into the alteplase vial. 3) Mix the solution with a gentle swirl to make sure the product is completely dissolved. 4) Visually inspect the solution for particulate matter and discoloration prior to administration. 5) Concentration after reconstitution = 1 mg/mL
particularity: Incompatible in Y with D5W, LR or any other solutions. Only compatible with NaCl 0.9%.  Contraindications (Non-exhaustive list, see monograph)  Absolute Contraindications: - Active internal bleeding - Within last 3 months: intracranial or intra-spinal surgery, severe head trauma, intracranial hemorrhage, prior ischemic stroke - Arterial or venous intracerebral malformation, aneurysm or cerebral neoplasia - Active coagulation disorder (e.g. Von Willebrand disease) - Suspected aortic dissection - Alteplase anaphylaxis  Relative Contraindications: - Current use of anticoagulants (heparin (< 48 hrs with abnormal aPTT), enoxaparin (< 24 hrs), dalteparin (< 24 hrs), warfarin, dabigatran, rivaroxaban, apixaban, edoxaban) - Pregnancy - Traumatic CPR - Internal bleed within 4 weeks prior - Systolic BP >99th percentile or > 180 mmHg despite treatment - Major surgery within 3 weeks prior - Ischemic stroke > 3 months old

administration: The Amox/Clav 5:1 formulation is not indicated for community acquired pneumonia or other suspected pneumococcal infections. A single dose of 50 mg/Kg, max 2 g with the 5:1 formulation is safe for the patient, but to limit side effects related to the clavulanic acid dose, favor the use of the Amoxicillin 2000 mg/Clavulanic Acid 200 mg formulation (Amox/Clav ratio 10:1).  Reconstitution: 1) Presentation: Vial of Amoxicillin 2000 mg/Clavulanic Acid 200 mg 2) Add 20 mL of sterile water 3) Concentration after reconstitution = 100 mg/mL in Amoxicillin.  4) Draw the indicated dose and add to the indicated volume of NaCl 0.9%  Additional information: • If different presentation, reconstitute according to the manufacturer's recommendations. (The concentration after reconstitution of the 5:1 formulation is 50 mg/mL.)  • The primary dilution is stable 15 minutes after dilution. • The primary dilution may be slightly pink, this coloration should disappear after shaking. • The maximum concentration for administration, after further dilution, is 20 mg/mL.
concentration_note: Dose expressed in mg of amoxicillin
particularity: Incompatible with D5W. Y-site compatible with NaCl, LR.  The first dose of an anti-infective medication (mg) should not be influenced by the presence or absence of renal insufficiency in order to achieve an effective concentration quickly. If adjustments are necessary, these should be applied at subsequent doses.  Usual dose including pneumococcus (10:1 formulation): > 3 months of life: 50 mg/Kg/dose IV q8h up to a maximum of 2g IV q8h < 3 months of life: 50 mg/Kg/dose IV q12h excluding premature patients and patients with reduced renal function.  Usual dosage (e.g.: non-severe ENT infections, cystitis) (Formulation 10:1 or 5:1) > 3 months of life: 25 mg/Kg/dose IV q8h up to a maximum of 1g IV q8h < 3 months of life: 25 mg/Kg/dose IV q12h excluding premature patients and patients with reduced renal function.  Dose adjustment in renal failure. The 10:1 formulation has not been studied in renal impairment. In renal impairment, the clearance of amoxicillin is more affected than that of clavulanic acid. The ratio may possibly increase, so it seems safe to use the 5: 1 formulation.

administration: Two formulations of amphotericin B are available: deoxycholate solution (Fungizone®) and liposomal suspension (Ambisome®). These formulations are not equivalent: the properties, indications and dosages are not the same from one formulation to another.  Reconstitution: Presentation: Vial of Amphotericin B Liposome 50 mg 1) Add 12 mL of sterile water 2) Concentration after reconstitution = 4 mg/mL 3) Shake the vial vigorously for about 15 seconds 4) Take the indicated dose WITH THE 5 micron FILTER and add to the indicated volume of D5%  Additional information: • Final concentration between 0.2 and 2 mg/mL. • Do NOT filter during administration • Do NOT administer IV push.  If 25 mL bag not available 1) Remove 25 mL from a 50 mL bag. 2) Add the indicated dose. 3) Administer at the indicated rate. preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0,9% after reconstitution
administration: Reconstitute according to the manufacturer's recommendations. IV administration over 3-5 minutes is safe according to the literature. It is possible to administer the drug over a longer period according to the hospital protocols in place. Secondary dilution not necessary. Can be administered IM.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.   The first dose of an anti-infective medication (mg) should not be influenced by the presence or absence of renal insufficiency in order to achieve an effective concentration quickly. If adjustments are necessary, these should be applied at subsequent doses.  Usual dosing: 200 mg/Kg/day divided q6h up to 2g IV q6h (maximum adult dose for severe infection: 2g IV q4h) excluding patients <1 month and premature babies and patients with reduced renal function. Dosage adjustment in renal failure.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted, without a filter
administration: Repeat 5-10 vials as needed after 3-5 minutes   See the indications in Particularites  Reconstitution: Presentation: Vial of Anti-digitalis antibodies 40 mg 1) Add 4 mL of sterile water 2) Mix gently, roll between hands until completely dissolved to avoid bubble formation  3) Concentration after reconstitution = 10 mg/mL
particularity: Keep refrigerated Maximum total dose : 20 vials  For poisoning by digitalis derivatives other than digoxin, consult your poison control center.   For potentially lethal poisoning with digoxin or other substances containing cardiac glycosides (plants, digitoxin, bufo venom, etc.) with the following symptoms:  • Symptomatic progressive bradyarrhythmia • Atropine-resistant 2nd or 3rd degree AV block • Ventricular tachycardia or fibrillation • Digoxinemia > 12.8 nmol/L or > 10 ng/mL, more than 6 hours following acute ingestion without cardiotoxic coingestion for asymptomatic or midly symptomatic patients. • K+ ≥ 5 mmol/L during acute poisoning

particularity: For poisoning by digitalis derivatives other than digoxin, consult your poison control center.  For potentially lethal poisoning with digoxin or other substances containing cardiac glycosides (plants, digitoxin, bufo venom, etc.) with the following symptoms:  • Symptomatic progressive bradyarrhythmia • Atropine-resistant 2nd or 3rd degree AV block • Ventricular tachycardia or fibrillation • Digoxinemia > 12.8 nmol/L or > 10 ng/mL, more than 6 hours following acute ingestion without cardiotoxic coingestion for asymptomatic or midly symptomatic patients. • K+ ≥ 5 mmol/L during acute poisoning  Additional information:  • Y-site compatible with NaCl. For potentially lethal poisoning with digoxin or other substances containing cardiac glycosides (plants, digitoxin, bufo venom, etc.) with the following symptoms:  • Symptomatic progressive bradyarrhythmia • Atropine-resistant 2nd or 3rd degree AV block • Ventricular tachycardia or fibrillation • Digoxinemia > 12.8 nmol/L or 10 ng/mL, more than 6 hours following acute ingestion without cardiotoxic coingestion for asymptomatic or midly symptomatic patients. • K+ ≥ 5 mmol/L during acute poisoning    Additional information:  • Y-site compatible with NaCl. No compatibility data with other solutions or other medications. • Keep refrigerated. Stable 4 hours in the fridge after reconstitution. • Number of vials to administer per dose = digoxinemia 6 hours post ingestion (ng/mL) x weight (kg) / 100 x 25% (Maximum 10 vials per dose.)  • Measure digoxin level before administration of the antibodies as the level measured post administration is no longer reliable for at least 24 hours.

preparation: Administer DILUTED by pump or syringe pump
administration: DILUTED solution at 1 mg/mL:  Reconstitution: Presentation: Vial of Azithromycin 500 mg 1) Add 4.8 mL of sterile water 2) Concentration after reconstitution = 100 mg/mL  Further dilution for administration via pump or syringe pump: 1) Draw 5 mL of DILUTED Azithromycin at 100 mg/mL 2) Withdraw 5 mL from a 500 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% 3) Add 5 mL of DILUTED Azithromycin to the 500 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% 4) Final concentration after further dilution = 1 mg/mL 5) Administer the indicated dose via pump (Volume/time) or draw the indicated dose and administer via syringe pump.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  Usual dosage: 10 mg/Kg, max 500 mg IV q24h. No adjustment required in renal failure  Possible increase in QTc without an increase in the incidence of Torsade de Pointes.

administration: Administer preferably through a proximal IV access (Hyperosmolar solution)  If Sodium Bicarbonate 4.2% not available: Presentation : Sodium Bicarbonate 8.4% 1mmol/ml 1) Dilute 10mmol (10ml) with 10ml of sterile water or D5% 2) Final concentration = 4.2% 3) Draw the required dose 4) Administer IV push  Use sodium Bicarbonate solution at 1 mmol/mL (8.4%) for patients of 2 years old or more

administration: • Final concentration: 130 mmol/L • Do NOT remove volume during preparation • Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting the administration • Titrate the infusion to obtain a urinary pH greater than 7.5 • The dose of 0.26-0.40 mmol/Kg/h represents a significant amount of volume of 2-3 mL/Kg/h and can partially or totally replace the maintenance fluids.
particularity: Keep the serum potassium over 4 mmol/L Close monitoring of blood pH in addition to urine pH.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted
administration: IV push in cardiac arrest  If the patient is hemodynamically stable, administer diluted over 30 minutes to reduce the risk of cerebral hemorrhage and better efficacy. (Hyperosmolar undiluted bicarbonate causes K+ to move extracellularly secondary to the hyperosmolar effect, thus negating the effect on K+ due to pH)  Administer preferably via proximal peripheral venous access if central access not available (hyperosmolar solution).  Neonates often tolerate blood levels of K+ higher than children and adults. In doubt, control the K+ venous level and look for anomalies on the 12-lead EKG.  If Sodium Bicarbonate 4.2% (0.5mmol/mL) is unavailable: Presentation : Vial or syringe of Sodium Bicarbonate 8.4% (1 mmol/mL) 1) Withdraw 10 mL of Bicarbonate 1 mmol/mL with a 20 mL syringe 2) Add to 10 mL of Sterile water for injection or D5W to the syringe 3) Final concentration = 10 mmol in 20 mL = 0.5 mmol/mL = 4.2%
particularity: • Y-site compatible with NaCl 0.9%, D5%, KCl. Incompatible with Lactate Ringer. • Multiple drug incompatibilities. Sodium Bicarbonate inactivates vasopressors and catecholaminergic type inotropes (Ex.: Epinephrine, Norepinephrine) • Incompatible in Y with Calcium or Epinephrine. Rinse well with NaCl 0.9% between administrations • No benefit reported in the literature of undiluted hypertonic bicarbonates compared to isotonic bicarbonates in the treatment of hyperkalemia. • Potentially effective therapy only in the presence of metabolic acidosis. Does not replace other therapies known to be effective in hyperkalemia (insulin, B2-agonists, calcium, etc.). • If intraosseous administration, interference with the blood gas if drawn in the same access.

preparation: Administer IV DILUTED on volume/time pump
administration: Further dilution for final concentration at 130 mmol/L: Presentation: Sodium Bicarbonate Syringe or vial 8.4% (1 mmol/mL) 1) Add 150 mL of Sodium bicarbonate to a 1L bag of D5W, WITHOUT removing volume. 2) Final concentration = 150 mmol in 1150 mL = 130 mmol/L 3) Administer the indicated dose via pump (Volume/time) or withdraw the indicated dose and administer via syringe pump. 4) Rinse the tubing with the solution before starting the administration  • Can be diluted in sterile water for injection if patient hyperglycemic. • If the patient is hemodynamically stable, administer diluted over 30 minutes for better efficacy and to reduce the risk of cerebral hemorrhage in newborns. (Hyperosmolar undiluted bicarbonate causes K+ to move extracellularly secondary to the hyperosmolar effect, thus negating the effect on K+ due to pH) • Administer via peripheral or central venous access. (Isotonic solution) • Neonates often tolerate blood levels of K+ higher than children and adults. In doubt, control the K+ venous level and look for anomalies on the 12-lead EKG. • A dose of 1 mmol/Kg gives a fluid intake of 7.7 mL/Kg. If patient is hypervolemic, administer a dose of IV furosemide for complementary therapy of hyperkalaemia.

indication: Non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis (NAGMA) or 2nd to AKI
administration: • Isotonic bicarbonate solution at 130 mmol/L at this initial rate provides fluid intake according to pediatric maintenance needs (4-2-1 Rule) up to a maximum of 80-100 mL/h. • Adjust the rate according to pH, volume status and the clinical situation. • Addition of another Y-solute is not recommended, as the patient will receive 200% of their baseline fluid intake.  Further dilution for final concentration at 130 mmol/L: Presentation: Sodium Bicarbonate Syringe or vial 8.4% (1 mmol/mL) 1) Add 150 mL of Sodium bicarbonate to a 1L bag of D5W, WITHOUT removing volume. 2) Final concentration = 150 mmol in 1150 mL = 130 mmol/L 3) Administer the indicated dose via pump (volume/time) or withdraw the indicated dose and administer via syringe pump. 4) Rinse the tubing with the solution before starting the administration  • Can be diluted in sterile water for injection if patient is hyperglycemic.
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, KCl. Incompatible with LR. • Multiple drug incompatibilities. Sodium Bicarbonate inactivates vasopressors and catecholaminergic type inotropes (Ex.: Epinephrine, Norepinephrine) • Indicated only in non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis (NAGMA) or secondary to acute renal failure. Not indicated in lactic acidosis, diabetic ketoacidosis, etc. • Although the BICAR-ICU study (Jaber 2018) demonstrated a potential benefit with 4.2% sodium bicarbonate (0.5 mmol/mL), this solution is not available in Canada in bigger format than 10 mL. Given the complexity and the risk of error when preparing such a solution, it seems more appropriate to continue to use an isotonic bicarbonate solution as described above when clinically indicated. • The BICAR-ICU study did not include patients < 18 years old. (Expert opinion) • May cause hypokalemia and hypocalcemia. • If intraosseous administration, interference with the blood gas analysis if drawn in the same access.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0.9%
administration: Caution: • Opinion of Pediatric Intensive Care recommended • Last-line treatment in refractory vasoplegic shock. • Ideally administered via central venous access, otherwise administration via good quality peripheral access is safe if for a short period of time and with frequent checks of the access site.  Additional information: • In the presence of a clinical response, consider starting the infusion 1-2 hours after the bolus/test dose. • Pulse oximetry reading may no longer be reliable after administration of methylene blue. • Rinse the tubing before and after each injection to reduce the risk of thrombophlebitis.
particularity: Incompatible with NaCl, LR. Y-site compatible with D5W.   Avoid if suspicion of pulmonary arterial hypertension. Pay attention to interactions with serotonergic agents since severe toxicity can occur.

administration: Caution: Opinion of Pediatric Intensive Care recommended  Suggested titration: 1) Start infusion at 0.25 mg/Kg/h. 2) Titrate 0.25 mg/Kg/h q1h according to clinical response 3) Maximum dose of 2 mg/Kg/h. preparation: Administer IV DILUTED
administration: • Warning: in the absence of a central or IO access, use Calcium Gluconate  DILUTED solution at 20 mg/mL  Further dilution for administration via syringe pump: Presentation: Pre-filled syringe of CaCl2 10% (100 mg/mL) (1000 mg in 10 mL) 1) Withdraw 10 mL of CaCl2 100 mg/mL and transfer to a 50 mL syringe 2) Add 40 mL of NaCl 0.9% to the syringe 3) Final concentration = 1000 mg in 50 mL = 20 mg/mL 4) Withdraw the dose from the DILUTED 20 mg/mL solution and administer over 5 min.  • Consider treatment when ionized/measured serum calcium < 0.9 mmol/L with clinical signs of hypocalcemia such as Chvostek, Trousseau or Erb, signs of prolonged depolarization on the ECG (prolonged QT or AV block), impairment of cardiac function due to lack of contractility, convulsions, papilloedema or psychiatric symptoms.  • Total blood calcium is a poor marker of hypocalcemia. (If ionized/measured serum calcium is not available, a total calcium < 1.65 mmol/L is closer to 0.9 mmol/L ionized/measured than a total calcium < 2.1 mmol/L.) • IV push if cardiac arrest secondary to hypocalcemia, hypermagnesemia or hyperkalemia; systematic use in cardiac arrest is no longer recommended as it diminishes the incidence of return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC). • Administer over 30-60 minutes in a stable patient
concentration_note: Dose expressed in mg of Calcium Chloride
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl. Incompatible with LR or Ceftriaxone • Increased risk of hypocalcemia in patients undergoing extrarenal purification with Na citrate (intermittent or continuous hemodialysis, plasmapheresis), transfusions of packed red blood cells or fresh plasma, chronic renal failure, post-op thyroidectomy, premature or newborn without vitamin D supplementation, biphosphonate treatment or suspicion of DiGeorge (22q11, often accompanied by congenital heart disease) • In case of concomitant hypocalcemia and hyperphosphatemia, giving calcium may cause precipitation of calcium phosphate (calciphylaxis). The ideal treatment in this situation is treatment of the hyperphosphatemia. • In elemental calcium equivalents, 1 g of calcium chloride = 3 g calcium gluconate.

administration: Ceftazidime is not a first choice in the empiric treatment of hospital-acquired infections or Pseudomonas infections.   Reconstitute according to the manufacturer's recommendations. particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR. Dosage adjustment in renal failure.

preparation: Administer deep IM undiluted into a large muscle
administration: • To reduce pain associated with IM administration, ceftriaxone can be reconstituted by diluting with Lidocaine 1% without epinephrine.  • Presentation: Vial of Ceftriaxone 250 mg 1) Add 0.9 mL of sterile water or lidocaine 1% without epinephrine  2) Concentration after reconstitution = 250 mg/mL  • Presentation: Vial of Ceftriaxone 500 mg 1) Add 1.1 mL of sterile water or lidocaine 1% without epinephrine  2) Concentration after reconstitution = 350 mg/mL  • Presentation: Vial of Ceftriaxone 1000 mg 1) Add 2.2 mL of sterile water or lidocaine 1% without epinephrine  2) Concentration after reconstitution = 350 mg/mL  • Presentation: Vial of Ceftriaxone 2000 mg 1) Add 4.4 mL of sterile water or lidocaine 1% without epinephrine  2) Concentration after reconstitution = 350 mg/mL  • IM administration technique• < 1 year old• 25 gauge (≥ 2.2 cm (⅞ in)) • Dorsogluteus muscle (max 2-5 mL depending on muscle size)   • Vastus lateralis or gluteus anterior muscle (max 1 mL/injection site)  • 1 year and over• 22 to 25 gauge (≥ 2.5 cm (1-1 ½ in)) • Dorsogluteal muscle (max 2-5 mL/injection site depending on muscle size) • Deltoid, vastus lateralis or gluteus anterior muscle (max 1-3 mL/injection site depending on the size of the muscle)
particularity: No dosage adjustment in renal impairment.  Usual dosing: 50-100 mg/Kg/day divided q12-24h up to a maximum of 2g IV q24h excluding patients < 1 month old and premature babies.

It is a possible option when amoxicillin/clavulanic acid or 3rd generation cephalosporins (ceftrixaone or cefotaxime) are not possible options. It is possible to administer the drug over a longer period according to the hospital protocols in place. Secondary dilution not necessary. Can be administered IM.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.   The first dose of an anti-infective medication (mg) should not be influenced by the presence or absence of renal insufficiency in order to achieve an effective concentration quickly. administration: In infants at risk of necrotizing enterocolitis, assess the risks/benefits with your poison control center and a pediatric specialist.  Can be used in toxic ingestions of less than one hour without contraindications (including airway protection).   Additional information: • Shake vigorously before administration. Administer PO over 15 minutes or via nasogastric tube • Can be mixed with other liquids to improve the taste: Cola: volume ratio 1: 1 or Cherry syrup: volume ratio 6: 1 (charcoal: syrup)
particularity: The use of activated charcoal in sorbitol suspension is no longer recommended.  Substances for which activated charcoal is not or very rarely recommended: alcohols, cyanide, hydrocarbons, ions, lithium, heavy metals, corrosive substances.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted
administration: Ciprofloxacin is not a first choice in pediatrics in the empirical treatment of hospital-acquired infections or Pseudomonas infections. Consult an infectious disease specialist before initiating.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.   The first dose of an anti-infective medication (mg) should not be influenced by the presence or absence of renal insufficiency in order to achieve an effective concentration quickly. If adjustments are necessary, these should be applied at subsequent doses.  Administration to immature animals has been associated with arthropathies, which has limited its use in neonatology, pediatrics and obstetrics; arthropathy has not been reported to date in children treated with fluoroquinolones, but clinical follow-up remains limited; muscular, peripheral nervous or central adverse reactions and tendinopathies have been reported in the pediatric and adult population.  Usual dosing: 20-30 mg/Kg/day divided q8-12h up to a maximum of 400 mg IV q8h excluding patients <1 month and premature babies and patients with reduced renal function. Dosage adjustment according to renal fonction and clinical indication.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted directly from the vial
administration: Caution: • Avoid if suspected congenital abnormality of lipid metabolism or history of anaphylaxis to eggs or soy. • Use with caution if patient with pancreatitis or severe acute hepatic failure.  Suggested titration: 1) Start infusion at 0.25-0.5 mcg/Kg/min 2) Titrate 0.25-0.5 mcg/Kg/min q5min until target BP is reached (onset of action 2-4 min; rapid titration q5min or more gradual q10min) 3) Maximum dose of 10 mcg/Kg/min or 350 mcg/min, i.e. 21 mg/h.  Additional information: • 20% Lipid emulsion: do NOT dilute • Use the bottle directly with a ventilated tubing • Rinse the tubing with the infusion before administration • Administer through a peripheral or central IV access.  • Very limited IV compatibility, preferably administered alone in one line. • The majority of patients achieve blood pressure control with doses < 5 mcg/Kg/min.
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR. • Discard the vial and infusion set or replace them after 12 hours when connected to the patient. • Protect from light during storage; not necessary when administered to patient. • Store in the refrigerator; stable for 2 months when placed at room temperature • Metabolized by plasma esterases; no adjustment for renal insufficiency. • The monograph mentions the potential negative inotropic effects of clevidipine without providing clinical data; adult and pediatric clinical studies, particularly post-op of cardiac surgeries, do not demonstrate these effects and the specific dihydropyridine mechanism of action, like nicardipine, does not suggest negative inotropy with clevidipine at therapeutic doses. • A dose of 1 mcg/Kg/min provides approximately 5.75 Kcal/Kg/day in the form of 20% lipids. The maximum adult dose of 21 mg/h provides approximately 2000 Kcal/day. Monitor triglycerides if high doses for > 24 hours, especially if administered concurrently with propofol or parenteral nutrition. (Expert opinion)

administration: Administer dose ≤ 300 mg over 10 minutes, dose 301 to 600 mg over 20 minutes and dose 600 to 900 mg over 30 minutes. It is possible to administer the drug over a longer period according to existing hospital protocols. Can be administered IM if undiluted (150 mg/mL)  If 25 mL bag not available 1) Remove 25 mL from a 50 mL bag. 2) Add the indicated dose. 3) Administer at the indicated rate.  Consider that the use of a bag of 25-50 mL can represent a significant amount of volume depending on the clinical situation.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  If using as an adjunctive protein synthesis inhibitor for toxin removal, always combine with a penicillin or cephalosporin. preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0,9% after reconstitution
administration: If irritation occurs during administration, dilute in a larger volume (minimum dilution to 100 mg/mL)  Reconstitute according to the manufacturer's recommendations. IV administration over 3-5 minutes is safe according to the literature. Secondary dilution not necessary if primary dilution to 100 mg/mL. It is possible to administer the drug over a longer period according to the hospital protocols in place. Can be administered IM.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.   The first dose of an anti-infective medication (mg) should not be influenced by the presence or absence of renal insufficiency in order to achieve an effective concentration quickly. If adjustments are necessary, these should be applied at subsequent doses.  Usual dosing: 200 mg/Kg/day divided q4-6h up to a maximum of 2g IV q4h excluding patients <1 month and premature babies and patients with reduced renal function. Dosage adjustment in renal failure.

preparation: Administer undiluted after reconstitution on volume/time pump
administration: • Pediatric doses of Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (4-Factor) for the reversal of direct oral anticoagulants (including dabigatran if idarucizumab not available) are poorly described in the literature and are mostly extrapolated from adult literature. (Expert opinion) • In non-life threatening bleeding, consult Pediatric Hematology for management.  Reconstitution:  Presentation: vial of PCC, provided vial of diluent (sterile water for injection) and Mix2Vial® transfer device. Refer to the monograph for detailed reconstitution. 1) Mix the PCC vial with the diluent using the device provided. DO NOT shake the bottle. 2) Gently swirl the bottle to make sure the product is completely dissolved 3) Concentration after reconstitution = 25 units/mL
particularity: Do NOT administer in Y with D5%, NaCl, LR or blood products.  Contraindications: - Prior heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) - Heparin allergy

indication: Vitamin K antagonist (VKA) reversal in life-threatening bleeding
preparation: Administer undiluted after reconstitution on volume/time pump
administration: • Must be administered with IV Vitamin K in case of major bleeding. • Pediatric doses of Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (4-Factor) for the reversal of anti-vitamin K anticoagulants are poorly described in the literature and are mostly extrapolated from adult literature. (Expert opinion) • In non-life threatening bleeding, consult Pediatric Hematology for management. • Reversal with Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (4-Factor) is faster and causes less fluid overload than fresh frozen plasma (FFP)  Reconstitution:  Presentation: vial of PCC, provided vial of diluent (sterile water for injection) and Mix2Vial® transfer device. preparation: Administer IV undiluted
administration: Contact your poison control center  Administer preferably through a proximal IV access such as an elbow crease or a central access. (Solution with pH at 9.5)  Diluting and preparing dantrolene can be laborious. This may require extra help and having someone dedicated do the preparation if needed.  Reconstitution: Presentation: Vial of Dantrolene 20 mg 1) Add 60 mL of sterile water 2) Shake the vial until the solution is clear. 3) Concentration after reconstitution =  0.33 mg/mL. 4) Draw the indicated dose and administer IV push undiluted as soon as possible.  This concentration is the only one available on the canadian market and involves a significant quantity of liquid.
particularity: Incompatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.   Continue the treatment q6h.  Consult your poison control center for discontinuation of therapy.

administration: DILUTED solution at 19,8 mg/mL:  Reconstitution: Presentation: Vial of Deferoxamine 500 mg 1) Add 5 mL of sterile water 2) Concentration after reconstitution = 94 mg/mL  Presentation: Vial of Deferoxamine 2000 mg 1) Add 20 mL of sterile water 2) Concentration after reconstitution = 95 mg/mL  Further dilution for administration via pump: 1) Draw 2000 mg of DILUTED Deferoxamine (4 vials of 500mg or 1 vial of 2000mg) 2) Withdraw 20 mL from a 100 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% 3) Add the DILUTED Deferoxamine to the 100 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% 4) Final concentration after further dilution = 19,8 mg/mL  Suggested titration: • Start the infusion at 15 mg/kg/h. • In the event of severe poisoning and in discussion with your poison control centre, the flow rate can be increased by 15-20 mg/kg/h every 15 minutes, depending on haemodynamic tolerance. Maximum dose of 50 mg/kg/h. • Usual maximum dose of 80 mg/kg per 24 hours recommended by the manufacturer. However, it is possible that a higher dose than this will be administered following consultation with your poison control centre.  Additional informations: • If the IV route is not available, the IM route can be used: 90 mg/kg initially, then 45 mg/kg per dose (max 1 g/dose) every 8h. For IM administration, reconstitute the 500 mg and 2 g vials with 2 and 8 mL of sterile water, respectively, to obtain a final concentration of 213 mg/mL.
particularity: Y-site compatible with NaCl, D5W, LR.  Indications: - Ingestion of 40 mg/kg or more of elemental iron with symptoms of toxicity. - Serum concentration greater than 90 mcmol/L (500 mcg/dL) in acute intoxication. - Serum concentration greater than 63 mcmol/L (350 mcg/dL) and cardiovascular symptoms or lactic acidosis.  If the appearance of adverse effects, such as hypotension, limits the titration of the infusion, consider the administration of vasopressors, especially for cases of poisoning requiring a maximum dose (50 mg/kg/h) of deferoxamine.

indication: Intracranial hemorrhage associated with antiplatelet agents
administration: IV administration without further dilution over 1 min should not be used for hematological indications of desmopressin, but only in diabetes insipidus.  Can be administered subcutaneously as a last resort, as subcutaneous absorption is erratic in patients in shock.
particularity: Keep refrigerated. Monitor for hyponatremia as well as IV intake and urine output. Desmopressin can have potent antidiuretic effect.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted directly from the vial
administration: Caution: • Pediatric Critical Care opinion recommended • Do not bolus, risk of severe bradycardia   If 4 mcg/mL commercial premixed vial not available:  DILUTED solution at 4 mcg/mL:   Further dilution for administration via pump: Presentation: Vial of Dexmedetomidine 100 mcg/mL (200 mcg in 2 mL)  1) Remove 2 mL from a 50 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% = 48 mL 2) Add 2 mL (1 vial) of Dexmedetomidine 100 mcg/mL to the bag 3) Final concentration = 200 mcg in 50 mL = 4 mcg/mL 4) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting the administration   Further dilution for administration via syringe pump: Presentation: Vial of Dexmedetomidine 100 mcg/mL (200 mcg in 2 mL) 1) Withdraw 2 mL (1 vial) of Dexmedetomidine 100 mcg/mL with a 50 mL syringe 2) Add 48 mL of NaCl 0.9% to the syringe 3) Final concentration = 200 mcg in 50 mL = 4 mcg/mL 4) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting the administration
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  Monitor for bradycardia and hypotension Dose up to 3 mcg/Kg/h possible under expert advice and continuous hemodynamic monitoring

indication: Adjuvant to high dose IV insulin in toxicology
administration: • Start only if blood sugar levels below 22 mmol/L and aim for blood sugar levels above 5.5 mmol/L. • D10% should only be used via the peripheral route in the absence of central access. In the presence of central venous access, use an infusion of D25%. Considering the significant flow rate of D10%, D25% via central access should be used as quickly as possible.  • In the hemodynamically unstable patient, low blood sugar should be treated by increasing the dextrose intake and not by decreasing the insulin dose. • The 2 active infusions (insulin and D10%) are usually at high flow rates and do not contain sodium. Consider close monitoring of serum sodium or the addition of a solute with sodium in Y.  If D10% is unavailable: Presentation : Vial of D50% 1) Withdraw 63 mL of D50% 2) Add to 500 mL bag of D5%  3) Do NOT remove volume during preparation. 4) Final concentration : D10% 5) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before administration  6) Enter the required volume on the pump and administer over 5 minutes.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5%, NaCl, LR and KCl

indication: Adjuvant to high dose IV insulin in toxicology
preparation: Administer IV DILUTED on volume/time pump
administration: Contact your poison control center  D25% should only be used via central access, particularly for the infusion. In the absence of central venous access, use D10%. Considering the significant flow rate of D10%, D25% via central access should be used as quickly as possible (smaller flow rate).  Further dilution for final concentration of D25% (prediluted D25% not available commercially): Presentation : Vial or syringe of D50% 1) Add 50 mL of D50% to a 50 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% (do NOT remove volume) 2) Final concentration = D25%-NaCl 0.45% 3) Draw the required dose and administer IV over 5 min  • Futher dilution with a 50 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% is used to simplify and reduce delays of preparation. If a D25% solution without NaCl is indicated (clinically unlikely), a dilution with sterile water for injection should be used instead of NaCl 0.9%. • Start only if blood sugar levels below 22 mmol/L and aim for blood sugar levels above 5.5 mmol/L. • In the hemodynamically unstable patient, low blood sugar should be treated by increasing the dextrose intake and not by decreasing the insulin dose.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR and KCl.

indication: Adjuvant to high dose IV insulin in toxicology
administration: Contact your poison control center  D25% should only be used via central access, particularly for the infusion. In the absence of central venous access, use D10%. Considering the significant flow rate of D10%, D25% via central access should be used as quickly as possible (smaller flow rate).  Further dilution for final concentration of D25% (prediluted D25% not available commercially): Presentation : Vial or syringe of D50% 1) Add 50 mL of D50% to a 50 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% (do NOT remove volume) 2) Final concentration = D25%-NaCl 0.45% 3) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting the administration  • Futher dilution with a 50 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% is used to simplify and reduce delays of preparation. If a D25% solution without NaCl is indicated (clinically unlikely), a dilution with sterile water for injection should be used instead of NaCl 0.9%. • Because of high flow rates, average patients will need a diluted bag of D25% approximately every 45-60 minutes. It is therefore preferable to have D25% prepared by pharmacy. If this is not possible, another option is to prepare a 250 or 500 mL bag of D25% with the above dilution. • For older children with high flow rates, consider having a more concentrated D50% solution prepared by pharmacy. If this is not possible, another option is to inject several vials of D50% into an empty bag and infuse at the same rate. (See EZResus Adult) • Start only if blood sugar levels below 22 mmol/L and aim for blood sugar levels above 5.5 mmol/L. • In the hemodynamically unstable patient, low blood sugar should be treated by increasing the dextrose intake and not by decreasing the insulin dose.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR and KCl.

preparation: Administer IV diluted up to 10 mL of NaCl 0.9%
administration: IV digoxin is not the first choice for treatment of pediatric tachyarrhythmias in general.  IV digoxin is not a first choice in children due to the lack of a pediatric IV formulation. Administer solely under the recommendation of a pediatric cardiologist with expertise in electrophysiology.  The 0.05 mg/mL or 50 mcg/mL pediatric IV formulation is discontinued in Canada; use the 0.25 mg/mL adult IV formulation for adolescents.  Choose an initial bolus of 0.25 mg or 0.5 mg.
particularity: After the initial bolus, repeat 0.25mg IV q4-6h for a maximum of 1.5mg total for the first 24h. Then, initiate the maintenance dose as clinically indicated.

administration: Suggested titration: 1) Start infusion at 5 mcg/kg/min 2) Titrate by increments of 2.5 mcg/kg/min q5-10min depending on clinical response. (Expert Opinion)  Additional information: • N.B. Dobutamine is a pure inotrope. Therefore, its efficacy cannot be evaluated with blood pressure changes, but rather by measuring cardiac output or by evaluating indirect signs of cardiac output. • Do NOT remove volume during preparation • Rinse the tubing with the infusion before administration
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  Monitor for hypotension.  Although the preferred route of administration of vasopressors is via central venous access, multiple studies demonstrate that administration via a good quality peripheral access is safe if used for a short period of time and with frequent verifications of the access site.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted
administration: If cardiovascular collapse (bradycardia, asystole, ventricular arrhythmias) secondary to the administration of a local anesthetic (bupivacaine, lidocaine, ropivacaine), when refractory to first-line treatments.  The loading dose can be repeated every 5 minutes (max 3 total doses) if there is no clinical response. Administer centrally or peripherally Filter not necessary during administration  Consult your poison control center if: - No response to boluses - Altered state of consciousness with or without convulsions secondary to the administration of a local anesthetic - Cardiac arrest or shock refractory to other therapeutic modalities secondary to intoxication with a sodium channel blocker or a fat-soluble drug. (To be considered only if all usual therapeutic modalities have failed.)  The administration of a lipid emulsion infusion is not supported by the literature.
particularity: Even if contraindicated by the monograph if allergy to eggs, soy or peanuts, the IV lipid emulsion does not cause cross-allergy or anaphylaxis according to the literature.  The majority of studies on lipid emulsion in intoxication have been done with Intralipid. The different lipid emulsions are not interchangeable in toxicology.  Administration of lipid emulsion may interfere with laboratory tests for several hours and cause occlusion of the ECMO or hemodialysis circuit.  Absolute maximum dose of 10-12 mL/Kg/24h

administration: Pediatric Intensive Care opinion recommended for children under 6 years old. For patients < 1 year, use another agent such as Nicardipine or Nitroprusside.  The 500 mcg/kg bolus is not generally used in pediatrics as children are more susceptible to the hemodynamic effects of beta-blockers.  Suggested titration: 1) Start infusion at 50 mcg/kg/min 2) Titrate infusion by 25 mcg/kg/min q5min as needed (Expert opinion)  Additional information: • Rinse the tubing with the infusion before administration.
particularity: Children, especially at a young age, are more sensitive to the hemodynamic effects of beta-blockers because their cardiac output is more dependent on their heart rate than adults. For this reason, esmolol is used only with expert advice.  Contraindications: uncompensated chronic heart failure, cardiogenic shock, bradycardia or heart block, asthma (uncontrolled)  Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  Short half-life (9 min)

preparation: Administer IV undiluted
administration: Fluconazole is not a first choice in the empiric treatment of invasive candidemia. See Caspofungin or Micafungin for empiric coverage of invasive candidemia.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR. Multiple drug interactions (CYP 450 2C9, 2C19 and 3A4), increased ALT and increased QTc.  The first dose of an anti-infective medication (mg) should not be influenced by the presence or absence of renal insufficiency in order to achieve an effective concentration quickly. If adjustments are necessary, these should be applied at subsequent doses.  Usual dosage: Loading dose of 12 mg/Kg/dose, max 400 mg IV followed by 6-12 mg/Kg/day, max 400 mg IV q24h, excluding patients <1 month and premature and patients with reduced renal function. Dosage adjustment in renal failure and dialysis.
