administration: • During methanol poisoning if the serum concentration is greater than or equal to 6 mmol/L (19.2 mg/dL). Folic acid or folinic acid can also be administered as an adjuvant.  • During ethylene glycol poisoning if the serum concentration is greater than or equal to 3 mmol/L (18.6 mg/dL). Pyridoxine and thiamine can also be administered as adjuvants. • When compatible history and symptoms AND metabolic acidosis with unexplained osmolar gap > 10, while waiting for a dosage.   If 25 mL bag not available: 1) Remove 25 mL from a 50 mL bag 2) Add the indicated dose  Consider that the use of a bag of 25-50 mL can represent a significant amount of volume depending on the clinical situation.
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl. Incompatible with LR (no data) • Stable for 24 hours at room temperature or in the refrigerator once diluted • If the solution has solidified in the vial (<25°C), it should be liquefied by placing the vial under warm running tap  water or warming by holding the vial in the hand.  Solidification does not compromise the efficacy, safety or stability of the product. • Discuss with your local toxicology center to establish the need for maintenance doses if IV route is not available and need to administer PO.

administration: Fomepizole is not a routine treatment for acetaminophen poisoning. Consider, after discussion with your local poison control center, in the presence of massive acetaminophen poisoning if hemodialysis is not available or late presentation.   If 25 mL bag not available: 1) Remove 25 mL from a 50 mL bag 2) Add the indicated dose  Consider that the use of a bag of 25-50 mL can represent a significant amount of volume depending on the clinical situation.
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl. Incompatible with LR (no data) • Stable for 24 hours at room temperature or in the refrigerator once diluted • If the solution has solidified in the vial (<25°C), it should be liquefied by placing the vial under warm running tap  water or warming by holding the vial in the hand.  Solidification does not compromise the efficacy, safety or stability of the product. • Does not replace Acetylcystein (NAC); administer concomitantly. • Indicated in severe acetaminophen intoxication, usually defined as one of the following: ingestion > 500 mg/Kg or > 30 g, 4 hour post-ingestion level > 5000 micromol/L or > 755 mcg/mL. • Consider in patients with choc, metabolic acidosis or altered mental status.

particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl. Incompatible with LR (no data) • Stable for 24 hours at room temperature or in the refrigerator once diluted • If the solution has solidified in the vial (<25°C), it should be liquefied by placing the vial under warm running tap  water or warming by holding the vial in the hand.  Solidification does not compromise the efficacy, safety or stability of the product. •  Does not replace Acetylcystein (NAC); administer concomitantly. • Indicated in severe acetaminophen intoxication, usually defined as one of the following: ingestion > 500 mg/Kg or > 30 g, 4 hour post-ingestion level > 5000 micromol/L or > 755 mcg/mL. • Consider in patients with choc, metabolic acidosis or altered mental status.

preparation: Administer IV DILUTED with equal volume of NaCl 0,9%
administration: Caution:  • Dose expressed in mg of "Phenytoin Equivalent" (PE) • Presentation: 50 mg/mL in Phenytoin Equivalents (PE) = 75 mg/mL of Fosphenytoin sodium. • Use Phenytoin OR Fosphenytoin. Do NOT combine the 2 therapies • If toxic seizures, do NOT use Fosphenytoin; use Phenobarbital or Levetiracetam.  Additional information: • Does NOT require using a filter. • Can be administered IM undiluted, but the large volume of administration may require up to 4 administration sites and IM administration is not recommended in status epilepticus. Favor the use of an intraosseous route rather than IM.
concentration_note: Dose expressed in mg of "Phenytoin Equivalent" (PE)
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  The use of Phenobarbital and Levetiracetam are preferred in status epilepticus in children < 6 months; therapeutic plasma levels are more difficult to achieve with Phenytoin. • Maximum administration rate: 3 mg PE/Kg/min (maximum 150 mg PE/min). • Fosphenytoin is rapidly metabolized to Phenytoin; aim for the same serum phenytoin levels and consider the same drug interactions.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0.9% with syringe pump
administration: • In the absence of a syringe pump or if availability of venous access is limited, it is acceptable to administer a first dose of furosemide over 2-5 minutes. • More risk of ototoxicity when administered in less than 10 min, patient chronically on furosemide or ototoxic drugs. • Patient naive to furosemide: 0.5-1 mg/Kg IV, max 40 mg • Patient already on furosemide: give at least the chronic PO dose IV. Maximal dose: 2 mg/Kg IV, max 80 mg except for furosemide challenge during anuria

The first dose of an anti-infective medication (mg) should not be influenced by the presence or absence of renal insufficiency in order to achieve an effective concentration quickly. If adjustments are necessary, these should be applied at subsequent doses.

administration: • Administer only in severe hypoglycemia and if IV dextrose is not available or in absence of IV access. Consider IO access to administer dextrose depending on the clinical situation, as glucagon takes at least 10-15 min to act. • Can be given IV, but if the IV route is available, dextrose should be given instead. • Hypoglycemia in pediatrics is usually ketogenic with lowered hepatic glucose stores; glucagon will be of little effectiveness in this clinical context.  • Effective in the treatment of insulin-dependent hypoglycemia.
particularity: • A 2nd dose can be repeated after 15 minutes PRN. • May cause nausea and vomiting, especially with repeated doses

preparation: Administer IM undiluted in the thigh
administration: • Extrapyramidal effects with haloperidol are more common in children (Dose dependent). The combination with diphenhydramine to prevent extrapyramidal effects does not clearly reduce its incidence according to the literature, but increases the risk of respiratory depression and lengthens the stay in the emergency room. Unless contraindicated, favor atypical antipsychotics such as olanzapine. • Repeat as needed up to a maximum cumulative dose of 0.1-0.25 mg/Kg/day or 10 mg/day • The 5 mg/mL lactate solution can be injected IV.  Do not use the long-acting 50 or 100 mg/mL decanoate solution IV.
particularity: • In the presence of extrapyramidal effects, treat with diphenhydramine. • Be careful if combined with another QT prolonging drug or in the presence of electrolyte disorders. • Depending on the clinical situation, consider the concomitant use of a benzodiazepine. Decrease the dose accordingly: increased risk of respiratory depression.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted after reconstitution with the provided 15 micron filter
administration: Repeat dose after 15 minutes if no improvement or partial improvement.  Presentation: - Vial: 5 g hydroxocobalamin - Sterile transfer device - Cardboard box to hang the vial - IV infusion kit with tubing and integrated 15 micron filter   Reconsitution: 1) Prepare 4 syringes of 50 mL of NaCl 0.9% 2) Reconstitute the hydroxocobalamin vial with this 200 mL of NaCl 0.9% WITHOUT using the sterile transfer device provided  3) Swing or flip the flask for at least 60 seconds to mix the solution (DO NOT shake) 4) Final concentration: 25 mg/mL 5) Hang the vial using the provided box 6) Use tubing and filter provided in the box 7) Administer via pump or gravity over 15 minutes  If the supplied filter is not compatible with your institution's pumps, administer by gravity or via DEHP-free tubing + 15 micron filter kit. (Do NOT delay administration. Confirm with your poison control center if in doubt)
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl. LR • Stable for 6 hours at room temperature after reconstitution  • The vial should not be shaken as this may cause foaming and make it difficult to control reconstitution. Since the reconstituted solution is dark red, some insoluble particles may not be seen. • If hydroxocobalamin is not available, administer sodium thiosulfate in combination with sodium nitrite. • Interference with certain laboratory analyses using colorimetry (creatinine, bilirubin, etc.) may last 24 to 72h. In addition, blood samples can be falsely interpreted as hemolyzed. • Alteration (lower) of carboxyhemoglobin has also been reported.

indication: Life-threatening bleeding with use of Dabigatran
administration: Lack of literature with idarucizumab in children. Pharmacological data suggest an adult dose in children to neutralize the same amount of dabigatran. (Expert opinion)   1) Rinse the tubing before the first dose with NaCl 0.9% 2) Administer undiluted via syringe driver or by suspending the vial with tubing 3) Administer 2.5 g IV over 5 minutes 4) Immediately administer the second dose of 2.5 g IV over 5 minutes 5) Rinse the tubing after the second dose with NaCl 0.9% Total dose: 5 g
particularity: • No other medication can be administered in the same tubing concomitantly. • Risk of serious adverse events in patients with hereditary fructose intolerance attributable to the excipient sorbitol. • The literature also supports the use of Idarucizumab to reverse Dabigatran for surgery and/or emergency procedure. • Keep refrigerated.

preparation: Administer IV without further dilution
administration: Caution: the different C1-esterase inhibitor products are not interchangeable.  Refer to the monograph for detailed reconstructions.    Presentation: -Berinert® vial of 500 units -Diluent vial -Device Mix2Vial®   Reconstitution: Presentation: Vial of Berinert® 500 units  1) Mix Berinert® vial with the diluent using the provided transfer device. DO NOT shake the vial. 2) Reconstitute each 500 units vial with 10 mL sterile water  3) Concentration after reconstitution = 50 units/mL 4) Gently swirl the Berinert® vial to ensure the product is fully dissolved 5) Visually inspect the solution for particulate matter and discoloration prior to administration.  6) Draw the desired dose using a syringe   Max infusion rate = 200 units/min
particularity: • Keep at room temperature. • Reconstituted vial stable for 8 hours • Indicated in acute abdominal, facial or laryngeal hereditary angioedema attacks.  • Therapies for angioedema should never delay securing the airway.

preparation: Administer IV DILUTED
administration: If premixed bag of insulin 1 unit/mL not available:   Presentation: Vial Insulin REGULAR 100 units/mL 1) Use a 250 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% 2) Add 250 units of insulin (2.5 mL of Insulin 100 units/mL) to the bag, WITHOUT removing any volume. 3) Final concentration = 250 units in 252.5 mL ≈ 1 unit/mL. 4) To mix the solution and saturate the adsorption of insulin to the PVC of the bag, vigorously invert the bag 10 times. 5) For a dose of 0.1 units/Kg, administer 0.1 mL/Kg of the solution diluted at 1 unit/mL over 1 min.  In order to reduce calculation and compounding errors, the final insulin concentration used is 1 unit/mL rather than 0.99 unit/mL. (Expert opinion)   Additional information: • In doubt, control venous K+ level and look for anomalies on the 12-lead EKG. • Administer with D10% or D25% to avoid hypoglycemia. (See D10% or D25% Adjuvant to IV insulin in hyperkalemia).  • It is possible to mix regular insulin and D10% before administration in paediatrics • Ratio of 0.1 unit of Insulin for 0.5 g of Dextrose to avoid hypoglycemia • Close blood glucose monitoring; post-insulin hypoglycaemia may occur up to 4-6 hours after the bolus of insulin, particularly in patients with renal impairment.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5%, NaCl, LR and KCl. Bag or vial stable for 25 days at room temperature when removed from the refrigerator.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0,9%
administration: • Always start a drip after the bolus • Administer with dextrose if blood glucose levels below 22 mmol/L (see D25% if central access available or D10% if peripheral access only) • In the hemodynamically unstable patient, low blood sugar should be treated by increasing the dextrose intake and not by decreasing the insulin dose. • Monitor potassium levels
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5%, NaCl, LR and KCl. Vial stable for 28 days at room temperature when removed from the refrigerator.
reference_snippet: - Canadian antidote guide in acute care toxicology [https://www.ciusss-capitalenationale.gouv.qc.ca/antidotes] - Engebretsen, Kristin. 2016. “Euglycemic Insulin Therapy.” In Critical Care Toxicology, edited by Jeffrey Brent, Keith Burkhart, Paul Dargan, Benjamin Hatten, Bruno Megarbane, and Robert P

administration: • Do NOT remove volume during preparation • Make 2 purges of tubing to saturate the PVC of the tubing with the insulin before connecting to the patient and starting the administration. • Do NOT prepare in a 50 mL syringe for syringe pump; excessive adsorption of insulin to PVC.  Suggested titration: 1) After the bolus of 1 unit/Kg, start the drip at 1 unit/Kg/h 2) Increase by 0.5units/kg/h every 15 minutes depending on clinical response.  3) Titrate to maintain a systolic BP and a pulse above lower age-set target.  Additional information: • Administer with dextrose if blood glucose levels below 22 mmol/L (see D25% if central access available or D10% if peripheral access only) • In the hemodynamically unstable patient, low blood sugar should be treated by increasing the dextrose intake and not by decreasing the insulin dose. • When the dose exceeds 2 units/Kg/h or before if clinical signs of fluid overload, it is recommended to transition to the higher concentration preparation of insulin (16unitsmL). • Monitor potassium levels
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5%, NaCl, LR and KCl. Vial stable for 28 days at room temperature when removed from the refrigerator.

administration: In pediatric diabetic ketoacidosis, the 0.05 units/kg/h insulin dose has been shown to be equivalent to the 0.1 units/kg/h dose over the duration of the acidosis and causes less hypokalemia. Reserve the 0.1 units/kg/h insulin dose for refractory cases or special situations in collaboration with pediatric endocrinology or pediatric intensive care.   Additional information: • Do NOT remove volume during preparation • Make 2 purges of tubing to saturate the PVC of the tubing with the insulin before connecting to the patient and starting the administration. • Do NOT prepare in a 50 mL syringe for syringe pump; excessive adsorption of insulin to PVC.
particularity: Treatment of acidosis related to ketone bodies: consider IV dextrose in case of hypoglycemia instead of decreasing insulin infusion.  Y-site compatible with D5%, NaCl, LR.  Keep refrigerated.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0.9%
administration: Do not give faster than 0.1 mg/Kg/min or 10 mg/min.
particularity: Children, especially at a young age, are more sensitive to the hemodynamic effects of beta-blockers because their cardiac output is more dependent on their heart rate than adults. For this reason, labetalol is used on expert advice, and the use of continuous infusion is rarely used in pediatric practice. (demi-vie = 5-8 hours)  Contraindications: Uncompensated chronic heart failure, cardiogenic shock, bradycardia ou heart block, asthma (uncontrolled)  Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR. The maximal hypotensive effect occurs 5-15 min after administration. Single dose can be doubled to a maximum of 1 mg/Kg/dose or 80 mg/dose if lack of clinical response.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0,9%
administration: Reduce the rate of administration if the patient is hypertensive, because leucovorin contains calcium.   If anionic gap without metabolic acidosis: 1 mg/kg. Repeat the dose every 6 hours until the intoxication resolves.  If anionic gap with metabolic acidosis: 2 mg/kg. Repeat the dose every 4-6 hours until the intoxication resolves and the metabolic acidosis is corrected. Adjust the dose and the interval according to the severity of the acidosis and history (denutrition, alcoholism, etc.).
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR • Keep refrigerated • Protect from light

administration: For doses < 375 mg, dilution with a 25 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% gives a concentration < 15 mg/mL (minimal documented concentration in the literature), but allows administration in the absence of a syringe pump. If syringe pump available, favor this method of administration for patients < 6.25 kg.   Administer doses < 3000 mg over 5 minutes and doses between 3000 and 4500 mg over 10 minutes.  If 25 mL bag not available 1) Remove 25 mL from a 50 mL bag 2) Add the indicated dose  Consider that the use of a bag of 25-50 mL can represent a significant amount of volume according to the clinical situation.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.   The use of Phenobarbital is preferred in status epilepticus in infants <4 weeks old.  Subsequent doses of 10-30 mg/Kg (Adult max 1500 mg) IV q12h can be administered if required. Adjust according to renal function. These doses can also be administered orally as bioavailability is close to 100%.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted
administration: Levofloxacin is not a first choice in pediatrics in the empiric treatment of community or hospital-acquired infections or Pseudomonas infections. Consult an infectious disease specialist before initiating.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.   The first dose of an anti-infective medication (mg) should not be influenced by the presence or absence of renal insufficiency in order to achieve an effective concentration quickly. If adjustments are necessary, these should be applied at subsequent doses.  Administration to immature animals has been associated with arthropathies, which has limited its use in neonatology, pediatrics and obstetrics; arthropathy has not been reported to date in children treated with fluoroquinolones, but clinical follow-up remains limited; muscular, peripheral nervous or central adverse reactions and tendinopathies have been reported in the pediatric and adult population.  Usual dosage:  6 months to 5 years: 10 mg/Kg/dose IV q12h > 5 years old: 10 mg/Kg/dose, max 750 mg IV q24h  excluding patients with reduced renal function.

administration: Premixed commercial bag at 2 g/500 mL D5W (4 mg/mL)  If premixed bag not available Presentation: Vial or pre-filled Lidocaine syringe 2% = 20 mg/mL (100 mg in 5 mL) 1) Remove 20 mL from a 100 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% = 80 mL 2) Add 20 mL (4 syringes) of Lidocaine 20 mg/mL to the bag 3) Final concentration = 400 mg in 100 mL = 4 mg/mL 4) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before administration  Suggested titration: 1) Start the infusion at 20 mcg/Kg/min after at least one bolus 2) Administer an additional bolus of 1 mg/Kg as needed before each increase in infusion (Expert opinion) 3) Maximum dose of 50 mcg/Kg/min or 4 mg/min
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  • Adjust the dose of the continuous infusion in heart failure, severe hepatic function, hemodynamic instability. • No adjustment in renal failure suggested, but potential accumulation of metabolites. • Monitoring of plasma concentrations (target of 5-21 micromol/L or 1.5-5 mcg/mL) suggested if toxicity is suspected or prolonged infusion.

indication: Torsade de pointes (Polymorphic VT with pulse and prolonged QT)
preparation: Administer IV DILUTED
administration: Caution: Opinion of Pediatric Intensive Care recommended  If pre-mixed bag not available DILUTED solution at 100 mg/mL  Further dilution for administration via syringe pump: Presentation: Vial of MgSO4 500 mg/mL (5000 mg in 10 mL) 1) Withdraw 10 mL (1 vial) of MgSO4 500 mg/mL with a 50 mL syringe 2) Add 40 mL of NaCl 0.9% to the syringe 3) Final concentration = 5000 mg in 50 mL = 100 mg/mL 4) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting the administration  Further dilution for administration via pump: Presentation: Vial of MgSO4 500 mg/mL (5000 mg in 10 mL) 1) Remove 10 mL from a 50 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% = 40 mL 2) Add 10 mL (1 vial) of MgSO4 500 mg/mL to the bag 3) Final concentration = 5000 mg in 50 mL = 100 mg/mL 4) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting the administration  Caution: • Risk of hypotension related to the rate of administration; start the bolus in 10 minutes and, if hypotension, reduce the rate by half. Use push dose phenylephrine rather than push dose epinephrine if congenital long QT syndrome cannot be excluded. • If recurrence of Torsades de Pointes, repeat the bolus and start a continuous infusion. • The DILUTED solution at 100 mg/mL allows the administration of boluses and continuous infusion via the same solution.
concentration_note: Dose expressed in mg of MgSO4
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.

indication: Torsade de pointes (Polymorphic VT with pulse and prolonged QT)
administration: Caution: • Opinion of Pediatric Intensive Care recommended • Dose expressed in mg of MgSO4  If pre-mixed bag not available DILUTED solution at 100 mg/mL  Further dilution for administration via syringe pump: Presentation: Vial of MgSO4 500 mg/mL (5000 mg in 10 mL) 1) Withdraw 10 mL (1 vial) of MgSO4 500 mg/mL with a 50 mL syringe 2) Add 40 mL of NaCl 0.9% to the syringe 3) Final concentration = 5000 mg in 50 mL = 100 mg/mL 4) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting the administration  Further dilution for administration via pump: Presentation: Vial of MgSO4 500 mg/mL (5000 mg in 10 mL) 1) Remove 10 mL from a 50 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% = 40 mL 2) Add 10 mL (1 vial) of MgSO4 500 mg/mL to the bag 3) Final concentration = 5000 mg in 50 mL = 100 mg/mL 4) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting the administration  Suggested titration: 1) Start continuous infusion if arrhythmia persists despite 1 or 2 boluses of MgSO4 in the last hour. Avoid continuous infusion in the presence of renal impairment (CLcr <30 mL/min) 2) Start at 25 mg/Kg/h aiming for magnesemia of 1.5-2 mmol/L 3) Increase to 50 mg/Kg/h if target not reached after 6 hrs. 4) If magnesemia 2.1-2.9 mmol/L, decrease the infusion by 50%. 5) If magnesemia ≥ 3 mmol/L, stop continuous infusion 6) If weakness, drowsiness, bradycardia or dyspnea, sample STAT magnesemia and reassess continuous infusion. 7) Stop the continuous infusion after 24 hours.
particularity: The MgSO4 infusion can be prepared in NaCl 0,9%, D5W or LR. Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted with a 0.22 micron filter
administration: Additional information: • Administer preferably via proximal peripheral venous access if central access not available (hyperosmolar solution). • If osmotherapy is indicated to treat intracranial hypertension, favor the use of NaCl 3% as first line. Administration of mannitol is not recommended in the latest guidelines for the management of pediatric severe traumatic brain injury. (No studies with inclusion criteria) • Premixed commercial bag at 100 g/500 mL (20% = 0.2 g/mL) • Rinse the tubing with the solution before administration. • Mannitol solutions are stable at room temperature, but mannitol may crystallize when exposed to temperatures < 20 degrees C. Warm the solution to at least 25 degrees C before administration.
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR • Monitor for hypotension • Do not administer if blood osmolarity > 320 mOsm/L • It is recommended to install a urinary catheter to follow induced diuresis.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0,9% after reconstitution
administration: Reconstitute according to the manufacturer's recommendations. IV administration over 3-5 minutes is safe according to the literature. It is possible to administer the drug over a longer period according to the hospital protocols in place. Secondary dilution not necessary.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.   The first dose of an anti-infective medication (mg) should not be influenced by the presence or absence of renal insufficiency in order to achieve an effective concentration quickly. If adjustments are necessary, these should be applied at subsequent doses.  Usual dosing: 60-90 mg/Kg/day divided q8h up to a maximum of 1g IV q8h excluding patients <1 month and premature babies and patients with reduced renal function. Dosage adjustment in renal failure.

administration: DILUTED solution at 1 mg/mL:  Reconstitution: Presentation: Vial of Micafungin 100 mg 1) Add 5 mL of NaCl 0.9% 2) Shake gently, avoiding foaming the solution 3) Concentration after reconstitution = 20 mg/mL  Further dilution for administration via pump: 1) Draw 5 mL of DILUTED Micafungin at 20 mg/mL 2) Withdraw 5 mL from a 100 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% or D5W 3) Add 5 mL of DILUTED Micafungin to the 100 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% or D5W 4) Final concentration after further dilution = 1 mg/mL 5) Administer the indicated dose via pump (Volume/time) or draw the indicated dose and administer via syringe pump.   Additional information: DILUTED 1 mg/mL solution favored, because solution > 1.5 mg/mL requires central venous access
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  Usual dosage for < 4 months of life: 2-10 mg/Kg IV q24h based on indication. No adjustment required in renal failure.

administration: • Midazolam is not a first choice as an induction or co-induction agent in pediatrics. • The Canadian Pediatric Society does not recommend midazolam for intubation in newborns and favors fentanyl 3-5 mcg/Kg. • In children, use ketamine, etomidate or propofol • Consider another induction agent in case of hemodynamic instability • Dose used when combined with fentanyl. If used as the only induction agent, use 0.2 mg/Kg, maximum 20 mg.
particularity: Risk of hypotension; promote the early use of vasopressor agents (Push dose pressor such as epinephrine or phenylephrine or a temporary drip of norepinephrine) rather than the administration of fluid boluses only.

preparation: Administer by nebulizer diluted up to 2-5 mL of NaCl 0,9%
administration: • Study demonstrates ineffectiveness if administered with standard (jet) nebulizer vs mesh nebulizer. • In a pulmonary hypertension crisis in patients intubated before cardiac arrest, milrinone can be administered directly into the endotracheal tube followed by a flush of 1-2 mL (< 10 Kg) or 2-5 mL (≥ 10 Kg) of NaCl 0.9% and by manual ventilations with a bag. • Administrer at a minimal O2 flow rate of 1 L/min
particularity: • NO remains the treatment of choice for pulmonary hypertension in pediatrics; prioritize this treatment if available. • Despite significant systemic absorption, inhaled milrinone causes less vasodilator effect than continuous IV infusion, according to studies combining pharmacokinetics and cardiac ultrasound measurements. • In addition to pulmonary vasodilator effects, inhaled milrinone has positive inotropic effects. • Inhaled milrinone may be combined with inhaled epoprostenol treatment; alternating administration every 2 hours is recommended in this situation.
reference_snippet: - Elbaser IIA, El Aleem El Derie AA. Does Inhaled Milrinone Facilitate Weaning From Cardiopulmonary Bypass in Children with Congenital Heart Diseases Complicated with Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension? Turk J Anaesthesiol Reanim. 2020 Apr;48(2):127-133.  - Singh R, Choudhury M, Saxena A, Kapoor PM, Ju

administration: Suggested titration: Milrinone has a half-life of 1.9-3.2 hours; unlike beta-agonists such as epinephrine, its titration q5-15min is not recommended. In the absence of hypotension, start at 0.5 mcg/Kg/min and adjust upwards in 4-6 hours. (Expert Opinion)  Additional information: • Do NOT remove volume during preparation • Rinse the tubing with the infusion before administration • The loading dose of milrinone is rarely indicated outside the operating room. • Adjust the dose according to renal function • If significant fluid restriction, possibility of concentrating the infusion to 500 mcg/mL by adding 50 mg (50 mL of milrinone 1 mg/mL) to 50 mL of NaCl 0.9%.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  If patient with reduced dextrose intake, very at risk of hypoglycaemia or with a history of hypoglycaemia, dilute in D5% rather than NaCl 0.9%.  Monitor for hypotension.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0,9%
administration: Use the minimum required dose for adequate analgesia and repeat as needed.  If 2 mg/mL concentration is unavailable:  Presentation : Vial of Morphine 10 mg/mL 1) Withdraw 1 mL (1 vial) of Morphine 10 mg/mL with a 5 mL syringe 2) Add to 4 mL of NaCl 0.9% to the syringe 3) Final concentration = 10 mg in 5 mL= 2 mg/mL   OR  Presentation : Vial of Morphine 15 mg/mL 1) Withdraw 1 mL (1 vial) of Morphine 15 mg/mL with a 10 mL syringe 2) Add to 6.5 mL of NaCl 0.9% to the syringe 3) Final concentration = 15 mg in 7.5 mL= 2 mg/mL
particularity: Maximum effect after 20 minutes Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  Adjustment necessary in renal failure if repeated dose (favor hydromorphone)

indication: TET spell in Tetralogy of Fallot
preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0.9%
administration: Caution: • Opinion of Pediatric Intensive Care recommended • Use in agitated or painful patient with hypoxic Tetralogy of Fallot. • The decrease in heart rate and respiratory rate due to morphine reduces the right-to-left shunt and improves pulmonary blood flow in the tetralogy of Fallot.  If 2 mg/mL concentration is unavailable:  Presentation : Vial of Morphine 10 mg/mL 1) Withdraw 1 mL (1 vial) of Morphine 10 mg/mL with a 5 mL syringe 2) Add to 4 mL of NaCl 0.9% to the syringe 3) Final concentration = 10 mg in 5 mL= 2 mg/mL  OR  Presentation : Vial of Morphine 15 mg/mL 1) Withdraw 1 mL (1 vial) of Morphine 15 mg/mL with a 10 mL syringe 2) Add to 6.5 mL of NaCl 0.9% to the syringe 3) Final concentration = 15 mg in 7.5 mL= 2 mg/mL
particularity: Maximum effect after 20 minutes. Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  Adjustment necessary in renal failure if repeated dose

indication: TET spell in Tetralogy of Fallot
preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0.9%
administration: Caution: • Opinion of Pediatric Intensive Care recommended • Use in agitated or painful patient with hypoxic Tetralogy of Fallot. • The decrease in heart rate and respiratory rate due to morphine reduces the right-to-left shunt and improves pulmonary blood flow in the tetralogy of Fallot.  If 2 mg/mL concentration is unavailable:  Presentation : Vial of Morphine 10 mg/mL 1) Withdraw 1 mL (1 vial) of Morphine 10 mg/mL with a 5 mL syringe 2) Add to 4 mL of NaCl 0.9% to the syringe 3) Final concentration = 10 mg in 5 mL= 2 mg/mL  OR  Presentation : Vial of Morphine 15 mg/mL 1) Withdraw 1 mL (1 vial) of Morphine 15 mg/mL with a 10 mL syringe 2) Add to 6.5 mL of NaCl 0.9% to the syringe 3) Final concentration = 15 mg in 7.5 mL= 2 mg/mL
particularity: Maximum effect after 20 minutes. Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  Adjustment necessary in renal failure if repeated dose

administration: If 2 mg/mL concentration is unavailable:  Presentation : Vial of Morphine 10 mg/mL 1) Withdraw 1 mL (1 vial) of Morphine 10 mg/mL with a 5 mL syringe 2) Add to 4 mL of NaCl 0.9% to the syringe 3) Final concentration = 10 mg in 5 mL= 2 mg/mL  OR  Presentation : Vial of Morphine 15 mg/mL 1) Withdraw 1 mL (1 vial) of Morphine 15 mg/mL with a 10 mL syringe 2) Add to 6.5 mL of NaCl 0.9% to the syringe 3) Final concentration = 15 mg in 7.5 mL= 2 mg/mL    • For initial titration (patient on continuous infusion of morphine or q3h), repeat every 5-10 minutes as needed.  • If morphine IV q3h regular or PRN, start at 0.05 or 0.1 mg/Kg/dose. • If breakthrough dose of IV morphine (q1h PRN), administer 1/2 the dose of morphine q3h or the same dose as the hourly dose of the continuous IV infusion. administration: Caution: The administration of this infusion can represent a considerable volume in the newborn. Consider a concentrated naloxone drip when the administration rate exceeds 50% of maintenance fluids (Expert opinion)  Calculate the infusion dose based on the cumulative bolus dose 1) Calculate the dose corresponding to 2/3 of the cumulative effective bolus dose 2) Start the infusion to administer this calculated dose in 1 hour 3) Stop the infusion according to the clinical response and the half-life of the narcotic  Example: The patient wakes up after 2 boluses of naloxone: 1st bolus 0.5 mg, 2nd bolus 1 mg 1) Effective cumulative bolus dose = 0.5 +1 = 1.5 mg 2) 2/3 of this dose = 2/3 x 1.5 = 1 mg 3) Start the infusion at 1 mg/h  Additional information: • Do NOT remove volume during preparation.  • Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting the administration
particularity: • Infusion can also be started empirically at 0.2-0.4 mg/h. • If respiratory depression reappears, a new loading dose of naloxone should be given and this dose repeated as needed, or continue the infusion. • The duration of the naloxone infusion should be dictated by the clinical examination, but also by the type of opioid involved, its dose and formulation (progressive release or not).
reference_snippet: - Canadian antidote guide in acute care toxicology [https://www.ciusss-capitalenationale.gouv.qc.ca/antidotes]  - Neumar RW, Shuster M, Callaway CW, et al. Part 1: Executive Summary: 2015 American Heart Association Guidelines Update for Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation and Emergency Cardiovascular Care

administration: DILUTED solution at 0.04 mg/mL Presentation: Vial of Naloxone 0.4 mg/mL 1) Withdraw 1 mL (1 vial) of Naloxone 0.4 mg/mL with a 10 mL syringe 2) Add to 9 mL of NaCl 0.9% to the syringe 3) Final concentration = 0.4 mg in 10 mL = 0.04 mg/mL

administration: Suggested titration: 1) Start the infusion at 0,5-1 mcg/Kg/min. 2) Titrate by 0.25-0.5 mcg/Kg/min every 5-15 minutes until target BP is achieved (onset of action 5-10 min; rapid titration q5min or gradual titration q15min). 3) Maximum dose of 5 mcg/Kg/min or 250 mcg/min, i.e. 15 mg/h. *** Caution: BP can drop precipitously after the initial titration *** 4) If titration q5-15min, once target BP is achieved, decrease the infusion by half, then titrate again gradually as needed. 5) If BP falls below target, decrease the infusion to minimum rate or hold entirely, then titrate again gradually as needed.  (Expert opinion)  • Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting the administration
concentration_note: Premixed commercial bag at 40 mg/200 mL NaCl 0.9% (200 mcg/mL)
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5%, NaCl, LR.  • Protect solution from light at all times  • Drug available through Health Canada's Special Access Program (Not marketed in Canada)

administration: If premixed bag not available:   Presentation: Vial of Nicardipine 1 mg/mL (10 mg in 10 mL) 1) Remove 20 mL from a 100 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% = 80 mL 2) Add 20 mL (2 vials) of Nicardipine 1 mg/mL to the bag 3) Final concentration = 20 mg in 100 mL = 0.2 mg/mL = 200 mcg/mL 4) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting the administration  Suggested titration: 1) Start the infusion at 0,5-1 mcg/Kg/min. 2) Titrate by 0.25-0.5 mcg/Kg/min every 5-15 minutes until target BP is achieved (onset of action 5-10 min; rapid titration q5min or gradual titration q15min). 3) Maximum dose of 5 mcg/Kg/min or 250 mcg/min, i.e. 15 mg/h. *** Caution: BP can drop precipitously after the initial titration *** 4) If titration q5-15min, once target BP is achieved, decrease the infusion by half, then titrate again gradually as needed. 5) If BP falls below target, decrease the infusion to minimum rate or hold entirely, then titrate again gradually as needed.  (Expert opinion)
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5%, NaCl, LR. • Protect solution from light at all times  • Drug available through Health Canada's Special Access Program (Not marketed in Canada)

indication: Heart failure with coronary artery disease
administration: Suggested titration: 1) Start the infusion at 0.3-0.5 mcg/Kg/min 2) Increase by 0.3-0.5 mcg/Kg/min q15min if hypertension up to a maximum of 2 mcg/Kg/min. (Expert opinion)  Additional information: • Doses > 2 mcg/Kg/min may cause arterial vasodilation. Use other agents for this indication. (Nicardipine, Nitroprusside, Milrinone or Phentolamine) • The use of bags without DEHP is recommended to reduce the release of plasticizers. For this reason, administration via syringe pump is not advised, as syringes are typically made of PVC. • Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting the administration
concentration_note: Premixed commercial bag/vial at 50 mg/250 mL D5W (200 mcg/mL)
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR. Monitor methemoglobinemia if dose > 2 mcg/Kg/min or combination with NO. Avoid in patients with Intracranial Hypertension.

administration: Additional information: • Do NOT remove volume during preparation • Rinse the tubing with the infusion before administration • Protect solution from light at all times  Suggested titration: 1) Start infusion at 0.5 mcg/kg/min. 2) Titrate by increments of 0.25-0.5 mcg/kg/min q5min until target BP is reached or a maximum dose of 5 mcg/Kg/min or 200 mcg/min. particularity: The risk of cyanide toxicity increases with the use of doses greater than 2 mcg/kg/min over more than 24-48 hours or renal or hepatic failure.  Avoid in patients with intracranial hypertension.  Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.

administration: • For concentration of norepinephrine 64 mcg/mL, see other tabs (Infusion by syringe pump or by pump) for dilution; not available in premixed commercial bags at 64 mcg/mL. • Use the lowest concentration if a peripheral venous access is used. • Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting the administration   Suggested titration: 1) Start infusion at 0.05 mcg/kg/min 2) Titrate by increments of 0.02 mcg/kg/min q5min depending on clinical response. (Expert Opinion)
concentration_note: Premixed commercial bag at 16 or 32 mcg/mL (250 mL D5W)
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.   Although the preferred route of administration of vasopressors is via central venous access, multiple studies demonstrate that administration via a good quality peripheral access is safe if used for a short period of time and with frequent verifications of the access site.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0,9%
administration: • Favor the subcutaneous route. • Consider administering IV if severe intoxication or peripheral hypoperfusion.  • IV administration over 3 min may cause bradycardia; consider administering over 15 min in 50 mL of NaCl 0.9% or bradycardia supportive therapy for patients at risk. (Consider that the use of a bag of 50 mL can represent a significant amount of volume depending on the clinical situation.) • Do NOT confuse with the long-acting or depot formulation (LAR)
particularity: • Administer in combination with dextrose. • Contact your poison control center  • Repeat the dose q4h if necessary (expert opinion: shorten interval to q4h when the IV route is used). • Keep refrigerated and protect from light. Stable for 14 days at room temperature.

preparation: Administer IV DILUTED
administration: Additional information: The DILUTED solution between 4.5-5 mcg/mL allows the administration of boluses and continuous infusion via the same solution. The volume provided by multiple boluses is adequate even for fluid-restricted patients. preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0,9% after reconstitution
administration: Reconstitution:  Presentation: vial of Pantoprazole 40 mg  1) Add 10 mL of NaCl 0.9% 2) Concentration after reconstitution = 4 mg/mL 3) Draw the indicated dose  Pantoprazole can also be administered diluted to 0.4 or 0.8 mg/mL IV over 15 minutes.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR, Plasmalyte, KCl. Incompatible with octreotide. Administer maintenance dose 12h after loading dose.  In the context of the paucity of pediatric data with continuous IV infusion in the literature, the adult literature which demonstrates the non-inferiority of q12h boluses compared to continuous infusion, as well as the difficulty of quickly setting up multiple IV accesses in pediatrics, particularly in patients with hepatic impairment (routes for blood products, infusion of pantoprazole, other medications), the administration of pantoprazole as a bolus q12h is preferred.

administration: Reconstitution:  Presentation: vial of Pantoprazole 40 mg  1) Add 10 mL of NaCl 0.9% 2) Concentration after reconstitution = 4 mg/mL 3) Draw the indicated dose  Pantoprazole can also be administered diluted to 0.4 or 0.8 mg/mL IV over 15 minutes.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR, Plasmalyte, KCl. Incompatible with octreotide. Start 12h after the loading dose and administer q12h thereafter. According to some sources, the maintenance dose can also be administered q6h.  In the context of the paucity of pediatric data with continuous IV infusion in the literature, the adult literature which demonstrates the non-inferiority of q12h boluses compared to continuous infusion, as well as the difficulty of quickly setting up multiple IV accesses in paediatrics, particularly in patients with hepatic impairment (routes for blood products, infusion of pantoprazole, other medications), the administration of pantoprazole as a bolus q12h is preferred.

administration: Caution: Dose in million units (1 M units = 1 000 000 units)  Reconstitution: Presentation: Vial of Penicillin 1 M units 1) Add 3.8 mL of sterile water 2) Concentration after reconstitution = 0.25 M units/mL 3) Draw the indicated dose and add to the indicated volume of NaCl 0.9%  Presentation: Vial of Penicillin 5 M units 1) Add 8.2 mL of sterile water 2) Concentration after reconstitution = 0.5 M units/mL 3) Draw the indicated dose and add to the indicated volume of NaCl 0.9%  Additional information: • If different presentation, reconstitute according to the manufacturer's recommendations.  • The maximum concentration for administration, after secondary dilution, is 0.15 M units/mL (150 000 units/mL). • IV administration over 15 minutes or more. • Can be administered IM.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.   The first dose of an anti-infective medication should not be influenced by the presence or absence of renal insufficiency in order to achieve an effective concentration quickly. If adjustments are necessary, these should be applied at subsequent doses.  Usual dosing: 0,15-0,4 M units/Kg/day divided q4-6h up to a maximum of 24 M units/day divided q4-6h excluding patients <1 month and premature babies and patients with reduced renal function. Dosage adjustment in renal failure.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0.9%
administration: Suggested titration: 1) Repeat the loading dose q5-10min PRN until the target BP is reached or a total cumulative dose of 0.1 mg/Kg or 20 mg. 2) Maximum effect reached in 2 minutes, duration of action 15-30 minutes. 3) Always follow the loading dose with the infusion. (Expert opinion)  Additional information: Reversible alpha receptor blockade with a half-life of 19 min.
particularity: Consider the use of this agent for targeted blood pressure control in cases of pheochromocytoma or intoxication with a sympathomimetic (eg: cocaine). administration: Suggested titration: 1) Start infusion at 0.5-1 mcg/Kg/min 2) Increase by 0.5-1 mcg/Kg/min q15min PRN until target BP is reached. 3) Maximum dose 6 mcg/Kg/min or 666 mcg/min (40 mg/h). (Expert opinion)  Additional information: • Reversible alpha receptor blockade with a half-life of 19 min. • Do NOT remove volume during preparation. • Rinse the tubing with the infusion before administration. • There are no studies on the stability of the chosen dilution, but other dilutions (10 mg in 500 mL) result in excessive high flow rates and fluid intakes. Dilution recommended based on reported cases and historical use at CHU Sainte-Justine and SickKids Hospital in Toronto. Use immediately after preparation and discard after 24 hours.

indication: TET spells in Tetralogy of Fallot
preparation: Administer IV DILUTED
administration: Caution: Pediatric Critical Care or Pediatric Cardiology opinion recommended  Presentation: Vial of Phenylephrine 10 mg/mL (10 mg in 1 mL) 1) Withdraw 1 mL (1 vial) of Phenylephrine 10 mg/mL with a 1 or 3 mL syringe 2) Add to a 100 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% WITHOUT removing the bulk 2) Final concentration = 10 mg in 101 mL ≈ 0.1 mg/mL = 100 mcg/mL 3) Draw the required dose using a syringe 4) Clearly identify syringes with the correct concentration 5) Administer IV push q3min PRN to a target O2 saturation  Additional information: • Use after the fluid bolus in the patient with hypoxic Tetralogy of Fallot. • Repeat the dose q3min PRN until target O2 saturations are reached or total dose of 20 mcg/Kg. (Consider IV infusion of phenylephrine if partial or temporary bolus response.) • Systemic vasoconstriction reduces the right to left shunt and improves pulmonary blood flow in Tetralogy of Fallot.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.   Although the preferred route of administration of vasopressors is via central venous access, multiple studies demonstrate that administration via a good quality peripheral access is safe when used for a short period of time with frequent verifications of the access site.

administration: Caution: • Opinion of Pediatric Intensive Care recommended • Not recommended as a 1st line agent in all types of pediatric shock  Preferred concentration in children under 5 Kg = 20 mcg/mL (2 mg/100 mL) and 5 Kg or more = 100 mcg/mL (10 mg/100 mL). Use the lowest concentration if a peripheral venous access is used.  Suggested titration: 1) Start infusion at 0.1 mcg/kg/min. 2) Titrate by increments of 0.1 mcg/kg/min q5min until target BP is reached or a maximum dose of 2 mcg/Kg/min ou 200 mcg/min. (Expert Opinion)  Additional information: • Do NOT remove volume during preparation. • Rinse the tubing with the infusion before administration.
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  • Maximal reported dose up to 5 mcg/kg/min • Although the preferred route of administration of vasopressors is via central venous access, multiple studies demonstrate that administration via a good quality peripheral access is safe if used for a short period of time and with frequent verifications of the access site.

preparation: Administer IV DILUTED with 0.22 micron filter
administration: • Use phenytoin OR fosphenytoin. Do NOT combine the 2 therapies • If toxic seizures, do NOT use phenytoin; use phenobarbital or levetiracetam • The use of phenobarbital and levetiracetam are preferred in status epilepticus in children < 6 months; therapeutic plasma levels are more difficult to achieve with phenytoin.  DILUTED solution at 8.33 mg/mL:  Further dilution for administration via syringe pump: Presentation: 250 mg in 5 mL vial (50 mg/mL) 1) Use a 100 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% 2) Add 1000 mg of Phenytoin (20 mL of 50 mg/mL) to the bag, WITHOUT removing the volume 3) Final concentration = 1000 mg in 120 mL = 8.33 mg/mL 4) Flush tubing with dilute phenytoin solution 5) Install a 0.22 micron filter at the end of the tubing 6) Withdraw the indicated dose and administer via syringe pump 7) Rinse tubing with NaCl 0.9% after administration. (Compatible only with NaCl 0.9%.)  Additional information: • DILUTED phenytoin solution stable between 5 and 10 mg/mL in NaCl 0.9% • Maximum administration rate: 1 mg/Kg/min (maximum 50 mg/min). In pediatric patients between 50 and 60 kg, administration of the loading dose over 20 minutes (rate up to 60 mg/min) is acceptable to reduce errors, since hypotension is rare. (Expert advice)
particularity: Incompatible with D5W, LR. Y-site compatible with NaCl.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0,9%
administration: • Dose can be repeated every 20 minutes for a maximum of 2 mg in the first hour. • Do not administer if QRS > 100 msec.  • Do not administer in mixed intoxications or intoxication with a tricyclic agent. • May induce bradycardia. Treat severe and symptomatic bradycardia with epinephrine rather than atropine (anticholinergic). • Discuss with your poison control center. An infusion may be indicated in certain clinical situations.  • Can be administered IM or subcutaneously
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl. Incompatible with LR (no data) • Antidote available in Canada only through Health Canada's Special Access Program.  • Unlike neostigmine and pyridostigmine, physostigmine easily crosses the blood-brain barrier.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0,9% after reconstitution
administration: Caution: Consult an infectious disease specialist  Reconstitute according to the manufacturer's recommendations. IV administration over 3-5 minutes is safe according to the literature. It is possible to administer the drug over a longer period according to the hospital protocols in place. Secondary dilution not necessary.
concentration_note: Dose expressed in mg of piperacillin
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR (Newer formulation of Piperacillin/Tazobactam without Calcium EDTA).   The first dose of an anti-infective medication (mg) should not be influenced by the presence or absence of renal insufficiency in order to achieve an effective concentration quickly. If adjustments are necessary, these should be applied at subsequent doses.  Usual dosing: 300 mg/Kg/day divided q6h up to a maximum of 4 g IV q6h excluding patients <1 month and premature babies and patients with reduced renal function. Dosage adjustment in renal failure.

preparation: Rate varies according to indication
administration: Additional information: • Repeat according to clinical response • In early massive hemorrhage, favor transfusing blood products at a ratio of 1:1:1 (Plasma: Platelets: RBCs) • If INR > 1.8 AND absence of bleeding, opinion of pediatric hematology or pediatric intensive care recommended • 1 unit of FFP = approximately 250 mL • May be administered over 3 hours if patient is stable

preparation: Administration rate varies depending on clinical situation
administration: • Administer according to local protocols and the urgency of the situation. • 1 unit of FFP = approximately 250 mL
particularity: • The dose can be repeated 2-4 hours later if symptoms persist. • Therapies for angioedema should never delay securing the airway. • For hereditary angioedema, use FFP only as 2nd line if first line therapies are not available (C1 esterase inhibitor, icatibant, ecallantide). • Controversial in ACE inhibitor induced angioedema. Data limited to case reports.

indication: Vitamin K antagonist (VKA) reversal in patients with acute major bleeding
preparation: Administration rate varies depending on clinical situation
administration: Use only if prothrombin complex concentrates (4-factor) are not available or contraindicated since fresh frozen plasma takes longer to administer, may cause transfusion reactions and has been shown to be inferior in the literature.   Administer according to local protocols and the urgency of the situation. Can be administered flush with pressure bag in case of major hemorrhage.    Opinion of pediatric hematology recommended.  1 unit of FFP = approximately 250 mL
particularity: • Administer 20 mL/Kg ( up to 4 units) empirically, or 10 mL/Kg (up to 2 units) and repeat dose if INR remains above 1.5. • Measure INR 15 minutes after the end of plasma administration. • Complete reversal of warfarin with FFP typically requires 30 mL/Kg. • Must be administered with IV Vitamin K (5 or 10 mg depending on weight) in case of major bleeding. • Consider furosemide before, half way or immediately after plasma administration in patients at risk for volume overload. • Consider calcium administration or close monitoring with repletion of calcium as need.
reference_snippet: - Chai-Adisaksopha C, Hillis C, Siegal DM, Movilla R, Heddle N, Iorio A, Crowther M. Prothrombin complex concentrates versus fresh frozen plasma for warfarin reversal. A systematic review and meta-analysis. Thromb Haemost. 2016 Oct 28;116(5):879-890.  - Monagle P, Chan AKC, Goldenberg NA, Ichord RN,

administration: • Consider during poisoning by an organophosphate insecticide causing the presence of nicotine signs (e.g.: fasciculations, muscle weakness) or damage to the central nervous system (e.g.: coma). Does not replace the use of atropine in the treatment of muscarinic signs (e.g. bronchorrhea). • Exceptionally, in pulmonary edema or significant fluid restriction, pralidoxime can be administered undiluted over 5 minutes. The need for fluid restriction is rare in cholinergic intoxication, because insensible losses are increased and rapid IV administration can result in: tachycardia, laryngospasm, apnea, muscle rigidity and transient neuromuscular block. Consult your poison control center. • Follow the loading dose by the maintenance infusion.   Reconstitution: Presentation: Vial of Pralidoxime 1000 mg 1) Add 20 mL of sterile water 2) Concentration after reconstitution = 50 mg/mL
particularity: • Y-site compatible only with NaCl.  • Antidote available in Canada only through Health Canada's Special Access Program. • Administer the loading dose as soon as possible after poisoning. • Can be administered IM or subcutaneously • Reconstitution: Presentation: Vial of Pralidoxime 1000 mg 1) Add 3.3 mL of sterile water 2) Concentration after reconstitution = 300 mg/mL

preparation: Administer IV DILUTED
administration: Caution:  • Opinion of Pediatric Intensive Care or Pediatric Cardiology recommended • If the patient is hypotensive, it is possible to administer the bolus over 10-30 minutes. • Administration of bolus procainamide < 5 min increases the risk of severe hypotension.  • If still in arrhythmia, additional boluses up to a total maximum of 15 mg/Kg or 1000 mg is possible.  DILUTED 3.85 mg/mL solution:  Further dilution for administration via syringe pump: Presentation: Vial of Procainamide 100 mg/mL (1000 mg in 10 mL) 1) Use a 250 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% 2) Add 10 mL (1 vial) of Procainamide 100 mg/mL to the bag, WITHOUT removing volume 3) Final concentration = 1000 mg in 260 mL = 3.85 mg/mL 4) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting the administration 5) For a dose of 3-6 mg/Kg, administer 0.78-1.56 mL/Kg (max 26 mL) of the solution diluted to 3.85 mg/mL over 5 min  Additional information: The DILUTED solution at 3.85 mg/mL allows the administration of boluses and continuous infusion via the same solution. The volume provided by multiple boluses is adequate even for fluid restricted patients.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.   Use with caution in patients with heart, liver or kidney failure. Adjust the dose in hepatic or renal impairment. Do not use if AV block of the 2nd or 3rd or if presence of lupus syndrome. Do not use if in doubt about Torsade de Pointes and assess risks/benefits with cardiology if QTc> 500 msec. preparation: Administer IV DILUTED
administration: Caution:  • Opinion of Pediatric Intensive Care or Pediatric Cardiology recommended • If the patient is hypotensive, it is possible to administer the bolus over 10-30 minutes. • Administration of bolus procainamide < 5 min increases the risk of severe hypotension.  • If still in arrhythmia, additional boluses up to a total maximum of 15 mg/Kg or 1000 mg is possible.  DILUTED 3.85 mg/mL solution:  Further dilution for administration via syringe pump: Presentation: Vial of Procainamide 100 mg/mL (1000 mg in 10 mL) 1) Use a 250 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% 2) Add 10 mL (1 vial) of Procainamide 100 mg/mL to the bag, WITHOUT removing volume 3) Final concentration = 1000 mg in 260 mL = 3.85 mg/mL 4) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting the administration 5) For a dose of 3-6 mg/Kg, administer 0.78-1.56 mL/Kg (max 26 mL) of the solution diluted to 3.85 mg/mL over 5 min  Additional information: The DILUTED solution at 3.85 mg/mL allows the administration of boluses and continuous infusion via the same solution. The volume provided by multiple boluses is adequate even for fluid restricted patients.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted directly from the vial
administration: Caution: • Avoid if metabolic disease suspected • Continous sedation with propofol is still a contraindication in children under 16 years old according to Health Canada, but the benefits can outweight the risks when used for less than 48-72 hours to avoid accumulation of other sedative agents such as midazolam or morphine. (neurologic assessment)  Additional information: • Lipid emulsion: do NOT dilute • Use the bottle directly with a ventilated tubing • Rinse the tubing with the infusion before administration  1) Start the infusion at a rate ranging from 30-50 mcg/kg/min 2) Titrate by increments of 5-10 mcg/kg/min q5-10 min until the desired level of sedation is achieved. (Expert opinion)  N.B. Increase starting dose and titrate faster depending on clinical picture
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR. • Propofol as a continuous infusion is not a first-line agent in pediatrics because of the risk of Propofol Infusion Syndrome (PRIS). If benefits > risks compared to therapeutic alternatives, make sure to measure lactates, CK, triglycerides and blood gas at least daily, as well as to reduce the duration of exposure to a minimum. The change in urine color, typically green, is not related to the risk of PRIS and is considered benign. • Use minimal required dose for adequate sedation. The use of a standardized tool for the evaluation of sedation depth in intubated patients is strongly recommended (ex: RASS). • Ensure adequate analgesia. • Monitor for hypotension.

indication: TET spells in Tetralogy of Fallot
preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0.9%
administration: • Use after fluid bolus and phenylephrine in Tetralogy of Fallot patient with hypoxia and tachycardia. • The decrease in heart rate as well as infundibular relaxation due to beta-blocker reduces the right-left shunt and improves pulmonary blood flow in the tetralogy of Fallot.  Caution: • Administer manually or via syringe pump over 10 minutes or until control of the TET spell.  • Avoid administration via volumetric pump (minimal volume of 25 mL), as it is too imprecise. • Start at 0.01-0.02 mg/Kg/dose and titrate according to the clinical effect (heart rate and O2 saturation) by doubling the dose q6-8h up to a maximum dose of 0.1 mg/Kg/dose or 1 mg/dose.
particularity: Contraindications: Uncompensated chronic heart failure, cardiogenic shock, bradycardia ou heart block, asthma (uncontrolled)  Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.

administration: In most cases of non life-threatening bleeding due to a heparin infusion, discontinuing the infusion alone is adequate.  Overcorrection with protamine can lead to paradoxical bleeding by inhibition of factor V.  The 0.5 mg/Kg up to maximal dose of 50 mg of protamine (expert opinion) is for patients with life-threatening bleeding and with ongoing or recent administration of therapeutic dose heparin only if it is impossible to calculate the exact dose of protamine (see below).   In case of less severe bleeding or if the administration of the anticoagulant dates more than a few hours, calculate the appropriate dose of protamine (see other specific tab) or consider administering a dose of 0.25 mg/Kg up to a maximum of 25 mg and repeating the dose only if necessary depending on clinical response (maximum of 1 mg/Kg up to 50 mg total. Experts opinion).   If 25 mL bag not available: 1) Remove 25 mL from a 50 mL bag 2) Add the indicated dose  Consider that the use of a bag of 25-50 mL can represent a significant amount of volume depending on the clinical situation.

administration: Overcorrection with protamine can lead to paradoxical bleeding by inhibition of factor V.  The 0.5 mg/Kg up to maximal dose of 50 mg of protamine (expert opinion) is for patients with life-threatening bleeding and with recent (≤8h) administration of therapeutic dose enoxaparin only if it is impossible to calculate the exact dose of protamine (see other specific tab).   In case of less severe bleeding or if the administration of the anticoagulant dates more than 8 hours, calculate the appropriate dose of protamine (see other specific tab) or consider administering a dose of 0.25 mg/Kg up to a maximum of 25 mg and repeating the dose only if necessary depending on clinical response (maximum of 1 mg/Kg up to 50 mg total. Experts opinion).   If 25 mL bag not available: 1) Remove 25 mL from a 50 mL bag 2) Add the indicated dose  Consider that the use of a bag of 25-50 mL can represent a significant amount of volume depending on the clinical situation.
particularity: Rapid administration may cause hypotension, a non-IgE mediated anaphylactic reaction or pulmonary hypertension.    Monitor anti-Xa 10 min after protamine is given, then again in 2-8 hours.   There is no literature to support the use of fixed-dose protamine in reversing LMWHs. This dose is an editorial choice, to be used only for the patient who is actively bleeding and who has received a dose of LMWH, but in whom the dose and the time of administration are unknown. The dose is extrapolated from unfractionated heparin reversal data.

The 0.5 mg/Kg up to maximal dose of 50 mg of protamine (Experts opinion) is for patients with life-threatening bleeding and with recent (≤8h) administration of therapeutic dose of low molecular weight heparin only if it is impossible to calculate the exact dose of protamine (see other specific tab).   In case of less severe bleeding or if the administration of the anticoagulant dates more than 8 hours, calculate the appropriate dose of protamine (see other specific tab) or consider administering a dose of 0.25 mg/Kg up to a maximum of 25 mg and repeating the dose only if necessary depending on clinical response (maximum of 1 mg/Kg up to 50 mg total. Experts opinion).   If 25 mL bag not available: 1) Remove 25 mL from a 50 mL bag 2) Add the indicated dose  Consider that the use of a bag of 25-50 mL can represent a significant amount of volume depending on the clinical situation.
particularity: Rapid administration may cause hypotension, an non-IgE mediated anaphylactic reaction or pulmonary hypertension.   Monitor anti-Xa 10 min after protamine is given, then again in 2-8 hours.   There is no literature to support the use of fixed-dose protamine in reversing LMWHs. This dose is an editorial choice, to be used only for the patient who is actively bleeding and who has received a dose of LMWH, but in whom the dose and the time of administration are unknown. The dose is extrapolated from unfractionated heparin reversal data.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0,9%
administration: Pyridoxine is not a routine treatment in status epilepticus but may be indicated in infants with suspected underlying metabolic disorder.  Can be administered IM.
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  • Ideally administer under EEG monitoring to objectify efficacy; do not delay treatment if EEG not available. • If seizures decrease/stop with Pyridoxine IV, consider Pyridoxine 50 mg PO BID. • Pyridoxine-dependent seizures are a result of defective binding of pyridoxine in the formation of GABA. They typically present in the neonatal period or early infancy.

administration: Caution: • Use only if patient has good air entry during intermittent treatments, but desaturates between treatments q20-30min. Continuous nebulization is not superior to intermittent q20min treatments. In case of deterioration without improvement with q20min treatments, switch to continuous IV salbutamol. • Fixed rate at 10 mL/h for optimal nebulization with all types of nebulizers. • By using the salbutamol solution for nebulization at 2 mg/ml, with a fixed flow rate of 10 ml/h, the dose administered is slightly higher than the doses usually seen in practice/recommended by the CSP (10 mg/h instead of 7.5 mg/h). This dose nevertheless corresponds to 0.5-1 mg/kg/h reported in the literature and ensures the feasibility of a simple dilution with the equipment usually available (Expert opinion)  • Administrer at a minimal O2 flow rate of 1 L/min, adjust the oxygen flow preventing the nebulizer from emptying or overflowing.   Further dilution for administration via pump if syringe pump not available: 1) Remove 25 mL from a 50 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% = 25 mL 2) Add 25 mL (50 mg) of Salbutamol 2 mg/mL to the bag 3) Final concentration = 50 mg in 50 mL = 1 mg/mL 4) Infuse directly into the nebulizer at 10 mL/h = 10 mg/h 5) Administer a 2 mL bolus into the nebulizer before connecting oxygen.   OR  For administration via pump if 2 mg/mL solution not available : 1) Remove 50 mL from a 50 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% = 0 mL or use an empty IV bag 2) Add 50 mL (50 mg) of Salbutamol 1 mg/mL to the bag 3) Final concentration = 50 mg in 50 mL = 1 mg/mL 4) Infuse directly into the nebulizer at 10 mL/h = 10 mg/h 5) Administer a 2 mL bolus into the nebulizer before connecting oxygen.   • Alternative dosage: See Intermittent nebulization and nebulize the 3 treatments q20min over 1 hour.
particularity: • Asthma in patients < 9 months of life is very rare • Ineffective in bronchiolitis

particularity: • Asthma in patients < 9 months of life is very rare • Ineffective in bronchiolitis

administration: Additionnal information: • Administer preferably via proximal peripheral venous access if central access not available (hyperosmolar solution) • Rinse the tubing with the solution before administration • If osmotherapy is indicated to treat intracranial hypertension, favor the use of NaCl 3% as first line. preparation: Administer IV undiluted on volume/time pump
administration: Administer preferably via proximal peripheral venous access if central access not available (hyperosmolar solution) Rinse the tubing with the solution before administration
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR. (Expert advice)  Administer in case of severe hyponatraemia with symptoms (seizures, altered level of consciousness, confusion, vomiting, tremors, movement disorders, etc.)  If there is no clinical improvement 5-10 minutes after administration, dose can be repeated. The biochemical objective is to increase serum sodium by 4-6 mmol/L in the first hours of treatment.  Monitor serum sodium closely thereafter, according to usual guidelines and with specialist consultation if necessary. Serum sodium should not increase by more than 6-8 mmol/L per 24 hour period. Risk factors for osmotic demyelination syndrome: serum Na <105 mmol/L at presentation, chronic/subacute presentation, hypokalemia, malnutrition, advanced liver failure or alcoholism (or history of).  If possible, before administration of 3% saline, measure serum and urine electrolytes/osmolarity.    To avoid iatrogenic hyponatremia secondary to maintenance fluids in pediatrics, it is recommended to avoid hypotonic solutions such as NaCl 0.45% and to use isotonic solutions such as NaCl 0.9% or, preferably, Lactate Ringer.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted after reconstitution
administration: • Not indicated in children < 10 years old or if onset of symptoms not defined or > 4.5 hours. • Validate the absence of contraindications with the neurologist before stroke thrombolysis. • Pediatric doses of tenecteplase and superiority over alteplase are based on extrapolation from adult studies and consensus of local experts including pediatric vascular neurologists and pediatric intensivists. If both thrombolytic agents are available, confirm the choice of agent with the neurologist; otherwise, administer the thrombolytic agent available as soon as possible. • Pediatric stroke usually requires radiological confirmation, either by CT or CT-angiography (normal parenchyma or with minimal ischemic change AND partial or complete occlusion of the corresponding artery) OR by MRI (acute stroke on diffusion sequences) combined with angio-MRI (partial or complete occlusion of the corresponding artery)  Reconstitution:  Presentation: vial of Tenecteplase 50 mg Refer to the monograph for detailed reconstitution 1) Using a 10 mL syringe, take 10 mL of sterile water for injection.  2) Inject gently the sterile water into the tenecteplase vial. 3) Mix the solution with a gentle swirl to make sure the product is completely dissolved, DO NOT shake 4) Concentration after reconstitution = 5 mg/mL
particularity: Y-site incompatible with D5%, LR or any other solutions. Only compatible with NaCl 0.9%.  Contraindications (Non-exhaustive list, see monograph)  Patient-related: - Weight < 40 Kg - Systolic BP >15% of the 99th percentile or > 185 mmHg despite treatment - Current use of anticoagulants (heparin (< 48 hrs with abnormal aPTT), enoxaparin (< 24 hrs), dalteparin (< 24 hrs), warfarin, dabigatran, rivaroxaban, apixaban, edoxaban) - INR > 1.7, platelets <100 x 10^9/L, aPTT > 40 sec, PT > 15 sec, fibrinogen < 1 g/L despite treatment - Active coagulation disorder (e.g. Von Willebrand disease) - Glycemia < 4 mmol/L or > 22 mmol/L despite treatment - Neoplasia (< 1 month after completion of treatment) - Anaphylaxis due to tenecteplase   Presentation related: - Onset of symptoms not defined or > 4.5 hours - Presentation suggestive of subarachnoid hemorrhage (even if CT-scan without bleeding) - Stroke related to infectious endocarditis, meningitis, moyamoya, sickle cell anemia, fat or gas embolism - Presentation suggestive of myocardial infarction or post-infarction pericarditis - Active internal bleeding  Related to imagery - Hypodensity > 1/3 cerebral hemisphere on CT-scan - Intracranial bleeding - Intracranial dissection (defined as involvement at or distal to the ophthalmic artery) - Arterial or venous intracerebral malformation, aneurysm or cerebral neoplasm  History-related: - History of vasculitis or vasculitis of the primary or secondary central nervous system - In the last 7 days: arterial puncture on a non-compressible site - In the last 14 days: childbirth, major surgery or biopsy of parenchyma - In the last 21 days: digestive or urinary bleeding - In the last 3 months: intracranial or intraspinal surgery, severe head trauma, intracranial hemorrhage, ischemic stroke

particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR • Usual dose: 2 loading doses of 9 mg/Kg/dose, max 400 mg IV q12h followed by 4-6 mg/Kg/dose, max 200 mg IV q12h • Multiple drug interactions (CYP 450 2C9, 2C19 and 3A4), increased ALT and increased QTc. • Adjust according to blood dosages (trough), aiming for 1 to 5.5 mg/L. • No dosage adjustment in renal impairment. In patients with CLcr <50 mL/min, the monograph does not recommend the use of IV voriconazole due to the potential accumulation of sodium sulfobutyl ether beta-cyclodextrin (SBECD), but the use <7 days in renal failure has not demonstrated any harmful effects.

particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR • Usual dose: 2 loading doses of 9 mg/Kg/dose, max 400 mg IV q12h followed by 4-6 mg/Kg/dose, max 200 mg IV q12h • Multiple drug interactions (CYP 450 2C9, 2C19 and 3A4), increased ALT and increased QTc. • Adjust according to blood dosages (trough), aiming for 1 to 5.5 mg/L. • No dosage adjustment in renal impairment. In patients with CLcr <50 mL/min, the monograph does not recommend the use of IV voriconazole due to the potential accumulation of sodium sulfobutyl ether beta-cyclodextrin (SBECD), but the use <7 days in renal failure has not demonstrated any harmful effects.

indication: Life-threatening bleeding with use of vitamin K antagonist
administration: • Administration of IV vitamin K with the current injectable formulation (without PEO-CO or cremophor) is safe and doesn't cause life-threatening reactions. • The use of subcutaneous vitamin K is not recommended for bleeding secondary to vitamin K antagonists because its absorption is erratic and unpredictable.  • Maximum rate of 1 mg/min to decrease the risk of non-IgE mediated anaphylactic reaction.   If 25 mL bag not available: 1) Remove 25 mL from a 50 mL bag 2) Add the indicated dose  Consider that the use of a bag of 25-50 mL can represent a significant amount of volume depending on the clinical situation.
particularity: • Onset of action (IV): 6 hours • Use in combination with Prothrombin complex concentrate (4-factor) or fresh frozen plasma (if 4F-PCC not available) • The dose of 5 or 10 mg IV is preferred to the dose of 0.03 mg/Kg for life-threatening bleeding, because the dose of vitamin K antagonist to be neutralized is adjusted according to the INR rather than the weight. (Expert opinions)

preparation: Administer IV DILUTED directly or via pump
administration: Caution: • For crashing anaphylaxis refractory to epinephrine IV with risk of arresting within the next minutes. Use the infusion without bolus if patient isn't pre-arrest. • 1 milliunit = 0.001 unit • Although the preferred route of administration of vasopressors is via central venous access, multiple studies demonstrate that administration via a good quality peripheral access is safe if used for a short period of time and with frequent verifications of the access site.  Concentration 200 milliunits/mL:  Further dilution for administration via syringe pump: Presentation: Vial of Vasopressin 20 000 milliunits/mL (20 units/mL) (20 000 milliunits in 1 mL). 1) Withdraw 0.5 mL (1/2 vial) of Vasopressin at 20 000 milliunits/mL with a 50 mL syringe 2) Add 49.5 mL of NaCl 0.9% to the syringe. 3) Final concentration = 10 000 milliunits in 50 mL = 200 milliunits/mL. 4) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting the administration or draw with syringe.
concentration_note: Same concentration for bolus AND infusion
particularity: The use of vasopressin bolus (push dose vasopressin) comes from limited anesthesia litterature on anaphylaxis.

administration: Caution: • 1 milliunit = 0,001 unit • 1 milliunit/Kg/min = 0,001 unit/Kg/min = 60 milliunits/Kg/h  • Although the preferred route of administration of vasopressors is via central venous access, multiple studies demonstrate that administration via a good quality peripheral access is safe if used for a short period of time and with frequent verifications of the access site.  Suggested titration: Increase in steps of 0.1-0.2 milliunits/Kg/min q15min depending on the desired BP target (Expert opinion)  Additional information: • Do NOT remove volume during preparation • Rinse tubing with the solution before administration
particularity: Risk of tissue necrosis and mesenteric ischemia, especially with doses > 4 milliunits/Kg/min or 40 milliunits/min; increased monitoring. (Expert opinion)  Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.

administration: Further dilution for administration via pump: 1) Choose the vials corresponding to the desired dose and according to availability (80 mg/4mL, 200 mg/10 mL or 400 mg/20 mL are all at a concentration of 20 mg/mL) 2) Add the total dose to a 50 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% 3) To mix the solution, gently invert the bag to avoid foaming 4) Administer IV over 1 hour 5) Finish by rinsing with 10 mL of NaCl 0.9%.  • Combined with dexamethasone, in patients with standard or high flow oxygen therapy, non-invasive or invasive mechanical ventilation with CRP > 75 mg/L. (RECOVERY and REMAP-CAP) (The benefits in mortality reduction have not been demonstrated with tocilizumab as monotherapy despite several studies.)
particularity: Tocilizumab has not been studied in randomized controlled studies in pediatrics for the treatment of acute COVID-19; its use is extrapolated from adult data and should be considered on a case-by-case basis, balancing the risks/benefits.  Contraindications: neutrophils < 0.5 x 10^9/L, platelets < 50 x 10^9/L, septic shock (not due to COVID-19), ALT > 5x upper normal limit, patient immunosuppressed or on immunosuppressive therapy , serious or latent infection (eg: tuberculosis), allergy to tocilizumab or one of its components.  Keep refrigerated.
reference_snippet: - Recovery Collaborative Group.  Tocilizumab in patients admitted to hospital with COVID-19 (RECOVERY): a randomised, controlled, open-label, platform trial. Lancet 2021;397(10285):1637-45. - Gordon AC, Mouncey PR, Al-Beidh F, Rowan KM, Nichol AD, Arabi YM, et al. Interleukin-6 Receptor Antagonists

indication: Severe infection including meningitis (syringe pump/fluid restriction)
preparation: Administer DILUTED
administration: Caution: A faster administration can lead to a Vancomycin Infusion Reaction (formely known as "Red Man Syndrome"). Consider giving over 90 min if history of reaction or dose of 20 mg/Kg.  DILUTED solution at 5 mg/mL Reconstitution: Presentation: Vial of Vancomycin 500 mg 1) Add 10 mL of sterile water 2) Concentration after reconstitution = 50 mg/mL  Further dilution for administration via pump: 1) Draw 10 mL of DILUTED Vancomycin at 50 mg/mL 2) Withdraw 10 mL from a 100 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% 3) Add 10 mL of DILUTED Vancomycin to the 100 mL bag of NaCl 0.9% 4) Final concentration after further dilution = 5 mg/mL 5) Administer the indicated dose via pump (Volume/time) or Draw the indicated dose and administer via syringe pump.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.   The first dose of an anti-infective medication (mg) should not be influenced by the presence or absence of renal insufficiency in order to achieve an effective concentration quickly. If adjustments are necessary, these should be applied at subsequent doses.  Usual dosage: 15-20 mg/Kg/dose IV q6-8h excluding patients <1 month and premature and patients with reduced renal function. Adjust the dose and the interval according to the indication and the blood levels, preferably targeting an AUC of 400-600 mg/L x hour or a trough of 10-20 mg/L. Dosage adjustment in renal failure.

preparation: Rate of administration varies depending on the clinical indication
administration: Additional information: • Repeat according to clinical response • In early massive hemorrhage, favor transfusing blood products at a ratio of 1:1:1 (Plasma: Platelets: RBCs) • If platelets low AND absence of bleeding, opinion of pediatric hematology or pediatric intensive care recommended • 1 platelets unit (apheresis) = approximately 250 mL
particularity: Dose for Massive Transfusion Protocol

preparation: Administer by volume/time pump or pressure infusion bag
administration: • If metabolic acidosis, hyperchloremia or hyperkalemia, favor a balanced solution such as Lactate Ringer's or Plasmalyte. Hyperlactatemia is not a contraindication for the use of Lactate Ringer's; 30 mL/Kg might possibly raise the lactate level by around 0.5 mmol/L. • For patients with intracranial hypertension, hypercalcemia or metabolic alkalosis, favor NaCl 0.9%. Plasmalyte (Na 140 mmol/L) can also be used in intracranial hypertension. • Reassess the patient between each bolus and initiate vasopressors early if repeated fluid boluses are required
particularity: Nil
