preparation: Administer by volume/time pump or pressure infusion bag
administration: • For dehydrated children with diabetic ketoacidosis (not in shock), administer a reduced bolus administered over a longer period of time to reduce the risk of cerebral oedema. • If metabolic acidosis, hyperchloremia or hyperkalemia, favor a balanced solution such as Lactate Ringer's or Plasmalyte. Hyperlactatemia is not a contraindication for the use of Lactate Ringer's; 30 mL/Kg might possibly raise the lactate level by around 0.5 mmol/L.  • For patients with intracranial hypertension, hypercalcemia or metabolic alkalosis, favor NaCl 0.9%. Plasmalyte (Na 140 mmol/L) can also be used in intracranial hypertension. • Reassess the patient between each bolus and initiate vasopressors early if repeated fluid boluses are required
particularity: Nil

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0.9%
administration: • For initial titration (patient on continuous infusion of hydromorphone or q3h), repeat every 5-10 minutes as needed.  • If hydromorphone IV q3h regular or PRN, start at 0.005 or 0.01 mg/Kg/dose. • If breakthrough dose of IV hydromorphone (q1h PRN), administer 1/2 the dose of hydromorphone q3h or the same dose as the hourly dose of the continuous IV infusion. (e.g.: IV hydromorphone at 0.01 mg/Kg/h => breakthrough dose of IV hydromorphone at 0.01 mg/Kg q1h PRN)
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR. • Hydromorphone 1 mg IV = Morphine 5 mg IV  • Use the minimum required dose for adequate analgesia.  • If possible, adjust analgesia according to validated tools in pediatrics. (numeric rating scale/FACES, FLACC, COMFORT-B)

preparation: q6h reg or PRN
administration: • Intra-rectal acetaminophen should only be used as a last resort, as absorption is erratic and bioavailability can be as low as 33%. • Contraindicated in severe hepatic impairment with impairment of function (INR, glycemia or albumin)
particularity: • The dosage of 15 mg/Kg/dose q4h PRN, maximum 5 doses/day is also possible, but may cause an interval of 8 hours without a dose. • Maximum dose of 75 mg/Kg/day to a maximum of 4 g/day

preparation: q6h reg or PRN
administration: • Do not use under 6 months of age, because of immaturity of platelet function and risk of closure of the arterial duct in newborns. • Do not administer intra-rectal, because of erratic absorption. • Contraindicated for patients with allergy to NSAIDs, moderate to severe dehydration, renal impairment, severe heart or liver impairment, active bleeding, gastrointestinal bleeding or ulceration, surgery or invasive procedure planned within 24 hours, coagulopathy, thrombocytopenia ( < 100 x 10^9/L), active varicella, anticoagulant treatment or chemotherapy in progress
particularity: Nil

preparation: Administer IM undiluted in the thigh after reconstitution
administration: Reconstitution: 1) Presentation: Vial of Olanzapine 10 mg 2) Add 2 mL of sterile water 3) Concentration after reconstitution = 5 mg/mL 4) Draw the indicated dose
particularity: • The combination with a benzodiazepine increases the risk of respiratory depression. • Negligible increase in QTc (4 msec). It is not necessary to do an ECG before administration. • The risks of extrapyramidal effects and neuroleptic malignant syndrome are less frequent with atypical antipsychotics such as olanzapine than with conventional antipsychotics such as haloperidol.  Contraindications: Serotonin syndrome → caution in case of poisoning (discuss with Poison Control Center) Tardive dyskinesia Congenital long QT syndrome (acceptable if acquired long QTc)

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0,9% after reconstitution
administration: • The injectable formulation of olanzapine is not officially approved for IV administration (like haloperidol), but is reported to be safe, equivalent to haloperidol in efficacy with fewer adverse effects. • Data on repeated use in children under 10 years of age, particularly with the IV form, are limited. Reassess indication before each dose considering potential accumulation.  Reconstitution: 1) Presentation: Vial of Olanzapine 10 mg 2) Add 2 mL of sterile water 3) Concentration after reconstitution = 5 mg/mL 4) Draw the indicated dose
particularity: • The combination with a benzodiazepine increases the risk of respiratory depression. • Negligible increase in QTc (4 msec). It is not necessary to do an ECG before administration. • The risks of extrapyramidal effects and neuroleptic malignant syndrome are less frequent with atypical antipsychotics such as olanzapine than with conventional antipsychotics such as haloperidol.  Contraindications: Serotonin syndrome → caution in case of poisoning (discuss with Poison Control Center) Tardive dyskinesia Congenital long QT syndrome (acceptable if acquired long QTc)

preparation: Administer IV undiluted on volume/time pump
administration: • Use the premixed heparin bags to administer the bolus; do NOT use concentrated vials at 1000 units/mL or more. • Administer the bolus after validation with a pediatric expert, if therapeutic anticoagulation is required within the first hour.  • Usual bolus: 75 units/Kg • Bolus in patient with significant risk of bleeding: 50 units/Kg • High dose bolus (congenital heart surgery like obstructed BT-shunt or ECMO-VA): 100 units/Kg  • Usual therapeutic target: aPTT 50-70 sec or anti-Xa 0.35-0.7 units/mL Favor, if available, adjustment according to anti-Xa, because better correlation with heparin levels with less interference from hemolysis, warfarin or liver dysfunction and less variations according to the reagent used in the laboratory. • Confirm the therapeutic target with the team, ideally before initiating therapy.  • If life-threatening bleeding on heparin, reverse heparin with protamine
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR, KCl.

administration: • Usual therapeutic target: aPTT 50-70 sec or anti-Xa 0.35-0.7 units/mL. (See Monagle et al in CHEST 2012 for detailed protocol for adjustments) • Favor, if available, adjustment according to anti-Xa, because better correlation with heparin levels with less interference from hemolysis, warfarin or liver dysfunction and less variations according to the reagent used in the laboratory. • Confirm the therapeutic target with the team, ideally before initiating therapy. • In children under 2 years old or in patients with prolonged heparin therapy, antithrombin deficits are common and may require higher doses of heparin to reach target. (> 40-50 units/Kg/h)  • If life-threatening bleeding on heparin, reverse heparin with protamine
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR, KCl.

preparation: Administer IV DILUTED
administration: In the majority of episodes in known patients with MELAS (Mitochondrial encephalomyopathy, lactic acidosis and stroke-like episodes), no imaging is urgently required and should not delay the administration of the loading dose of arginine.  DILUTED 26.8 mg/mL solution  Further dilution for administration via pump: Presentation: Vial of Arginine 250 mg/mL (7500 mg in 30 mL). 1) Use a 500 mL bag of D5W 2) Add 60 mL (2 vials) of Arginine 250 mg/mL to the bag, WITHOUT removing volume 3) Final concentration = 15000 mg in 560 mL = 26.8 mg/mL 4) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before starting the administration
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR, Plasmalyte, KCl. • Drugs to avoid or use with caution in patients with MELAS (non-exhaustive list, to be discussed with geneticist if necessary); triptans, phenobarbital, benzodiazepines, topiramate, propofol, neuromuscular blockers, aminoglycosides, salbutamol, metformin, statins, beta-blockers

preparation: Administer IV undiluted with syringe pump
administration: • Administer preferably via proximal peripheral venous access if central access not available (hyperosmolar solution). • If a syringe pump not available, administer on pump at the same rate directly from the vial. • Collect venous blood ammonia on ice to avoid falsely elevated values ​​due to haemolysis.
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR, Plasmalyte, KCl. • Medications to avoid or use with caution in patients with urea cycle disorder (non-exhaustive list, to be discussed with geneticist if necessary): corticosteroids, valproic acid and haloperidol.

preparation: Administer IV DILUTED
administration: • Administer preferably via proximal peripheral venous access if central access not available (hyperosmolar solution). • Collect venous blood ammonia on ice to avoid falsely elevated values ​​due to haemolysis.  DILUTED 9.09 mg/mL solution  Further dilution for administration via pump: Presentation: Vial of Phenylacetate/Benzoate of Na 100 mg/mL (10%) (5000 mg in 50 mL). 1) Use a 500 mL bag of D5W (bolus) or D10W (drip) 2) Add 50 mL (1 vial) of Phenylacetate/Benzoate of Na 100 mg/mL to the bag, WITHOUT removing volume, using the 0.22 micron filter provided with the vial. 3) Final concentration = 5000 mg in 550 mL = 9.09 mg/mL 4) Rinse the tubing with the solution before starting the administration
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR, Plasmalyte, KCl. • Final sodium concentration of 133 mmol/L. • Drug available through Health Canada's Special Access Program (Not marketed in Canada) • In hyperammonaemia secondary to hepatic insufficiency of origin other than urea cycle disorder, use Sodium Benzoate without Phenylacetate and Arginine. In both situations, for severe cases, Hemodialysis or CRRT should be use to clear ammonia. • Medications to avoid or use with caution in patients with urea cycle disorder (non-exhaustive list, to be discussed with geneticist if necessary): corticosteroids, valproic acid and haloperidol.

preparation: Administer IV DILUTED
administration: • For severe cases of hyperammonemia, hemodialysis or CRRT should be used to remove ammonia. Sodium benzoate can be used as a temporary treatment until the initiation of extrarenal replacement in a specialized pediatric center. • Drug not marketed in Canada in its IV formulation; the stock solution of sodium benzoate 100 mg/mL must be prepared in advance by the pharmacy from a powder. (preparation with filtration too long to allow treatment of hyperammonaemia in hepatic insufficiency in a timely manner.) • If sodium benzoate is not available, but phenylacetate/benzoate of sodium is, administer the bolus in the form of phenylacetate/benzoate of sodium pending transfer to a referral centre. (See entry Urea cycle disorder with hyperammonemia) • Administer preferably via proximal peripheral venous access if central access not available (hyperosmolar solution). • Collect venous blood ammonia on ice to avoid falsely elevated values ​​due to haemolysis.  DILUTED 9.09 mg/mL solution  Further dilution for administration via pump: Presentation: Vial of Benzoate of Na 100 mg/mL (10%) (5000 mg in 50 mL). 1) Use a 500 mL bag of D5W (bolus) or D10W (drip) 2) Add 50 mL (1 vial) of Benzoate of Na 100 mg/mL to the bag, WITHOUT removing volume, using the 0.22 micron filter. 3) Final concentration = 5000 mg in 550 mL = 9.09 mg/mL 4) Rinse the tubing with the solution before starting the administration
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR, Plasmalyte, KCl. • Final sodium concentration of 70 mmol/L.

preparation: Administer DILUTED after reconstitution
administration: Reconstitution:  Presentation: vial of Rasburicase 1.5 mg and provided vial of diluent (sterile water for injection) 1) Add 1 mL of sterile water 2) Mix the solution with a gentle swirl to make sure the product is completely dissolved. DO NOT shake the vial. 3) Concentration after reconstitution = 1.5 mg/mL 4) Draw the indicated dose and add to the indicated volume of NaCl 0.9%
particularity: • Incompatible in Y with D5W, LR or any other solutions. Only compatible with NaCl 0.9%. • Keep refrigerated. • Rasburicase continues to break down uric acid after blood is drawn; make sure to put the sample on ice until analysis in the laboratory. • Do NOT administer simultaneously with allopurinol for optimal transformation of uric acid into allantoin. • Rasburicase only treats the systemic and renal component related to uric acid; in the presence of hyperkalaemia, hyperphosphoraemia or hypocalcaemia, treat in parallel according to the usual algorithms. • Contraindicated if known G6PD deficiency. (methemoglobinemia)

(Bioavailability 100%) For patients with PRAM > 10, intolerant to dexamethasone PO or if in doubt about systemic absorption, because they are very symptomatic, switch to methylprednisolone IV. • Do not use commercial dexamethasone elixir because of high alcohol content. • If oral solution not available:• The 4 mg/mL IV formulation can be given PO. (Caution, PO formulation at 1 mg/mL and IV formulation at 4 mg/mL) • There is a compound oral solution of dexamethasone 1 mg/mL  • Consider a second dose 24h after the first dose.
particularity: Equivalent to 3-5 days of treatment with prednisone/prednisolone.

administration: • Do not use commercial dexamethasone elixir because of high alcohol content. • If oral solution not available:• The 4 mg/mL IV formulation can be given PO. (Caution, PO formulation at 1 mg/mL and IV formulation at 4 mg/mL) • There is a compound oral solution of dexamethasone 1 mg/mL  • Consider a second dose 24h after the first dose if symptoms remains.
particularity: • There is lack of evidence for the use of steroids in the acute management of anaphylaxis. Their use should not be systematic and could be reserved for certain cases (eg bronchospasm, upper airway obstruction, shock). The main treatment remains epinephrine. • The use of corticosteroids in anaphylaxis comes from evidence in asthma and the most severe and frequent clinical manifestation in pediatrics remains bronchospasm. The use of dexamethasone is based on the lack of evidence in anaphylaxis of corticosteroids in general, the evidence for dexamethasone in severe acute asthma, as well as the practicality of its administration compared to prednisone/prednisolone.

preparation: Administer by volume/time pump or pressure infusion bag
administration: • If metabolic acidosis, hyperchloremia or hyperkalemia, favor a balanced solution such as Lactate Ringer's or Plasmalyte. Hyperlactatemia is not a contraindication for the use of Lactate Ringer's; 30 mL/Kg might possibly raise the lactate level by around 0.5 mmol/L. • For patients with intracranial hypertension, hypercalcemia or metabolic alkalosis, favor NaCl 0.9%. Plasmalyte (Na 140 mmol/L) can also be used in intracranial hypertension. • Reassess the patient between each bolus and initiate vasopressors early if repeated fluid boluses are required

step_name: All critically ill children unless dehydrated (75%)
administration: Caution: Choice of IV solution to be determined  • Dextrose• Newborn < 72 hours of life: See maintenance D10% in paratus Neonatal • Term neonate 3-28 days old: D10%-LR or D10%-NaCl 0.9% (if NPO or at risk of hypoglycemia with low intake) OR D5%-LR or D5%-NaCl 0.9% (monitor closely glycemia and maintain > 3.3 mmol/L) • Patient 1 month of life up to puberty: D5%-LR or D5%-NaCl 0.9% • Pubescent patient (excluding hepatic impairment and prolonged fasting. Check blood sugar if in doubt): LR, Plasmalyte or NaCl 0.9%  • NaCl 0.9% vs Lactated Ringer's• Patient > 1 month old: do NOT use hypotonic solutions such as NaCl 0.45% • If metabolic acidosis, hyperchloremia or hyperkalemia: Lactate Ringer's or Plasmalyte. Hyperlactatemia is not a contraindication to the use of Lactate Ringer; 30 mL/Kg might possibly raise the lactate level by around 0.5 mmol/L. • If intracranial hypertension, hypercalcemia or metabolic alkalosis: NaCl 0.9% or Plasmalyte  • KCl 20 mmol/L• Not necessary in initial solute • Caution in oliguria, anuria, renal failure or acidosis • If in doubt, measure serum potassium before changing to a solution with KCl.  • Avoid manual additions of concentrated electrolytes, especially KCl in bag of IV solution. Use commercially available solutions.

preparation: q12h reg or PRN
administration: • Do not use under 6 months of age, because of immaturity of platelet function and risk of closure of the arterial duct in newborns. • Intra-rectal administration should only be used as a last resort, as absorption is erratic • Contraindicated for patients with allergy to NSAIDs, moderate to severe dehydration, renal impairment, severe heart or liver impairment, active bleeding, gastrointestinal bleeding or ulceration, surgery or invasive procedure planned within 24 hours, coagulopathy, thrombocytopenia ( < 100 x 10^9/L), active varicella, anticoagulant treatment or chemotherapy in progress
particularity: Maximum dose of 20 mg/Kg/day to a maximum of 1250 mg/day

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0,9%
administration: • Do not use under 6 months of age, because of immaturity of platelet function and risk of closure of the arterial duct in newborns. • Contraindicated for patients with allergy to NSAIDs, moderate to severe dehydration or shock, renal impairment, severe heart or liver impairment, active bleeding, gastrointestinal bleeding or ulceration, surgery or invasive procedure planned within 24 hours, coagulopathy, thrombocytopenia ( < 100 x 10^9/L), active varicella, anticoagulant treatment or chemotherapy in progress. • Can be administered IM. •  The solution for IM injection can be used safely for IV administration.
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5%, NaCl, LR and KCl. • The dose of 0.5 mg/Kg, maximum 15 mg/dose, can be repeated q6h. • The analgesic ceiling dose in adult is around 10 to 15 mg; higher doses do not provide more analgesia and cause more side effects. The analgesic ceiling dose in pediatric is not defined.

route: PO diluted in water, juice, milk or compote

administration: • Alternative dosage: 0.5-1.5 g/Kg, max 17 g PO q24h • 0.4 g ≈  5 mL (1 teaspoon) • If stools remain hard or grainy, it is possible to double the dose for 1-2 weeks. • If the stools are too liquid in a patient who is not constipated or bloated, it is possible to administer the dose every other day.
particularity: • Combine the pharmacological treatment of constipation with a diet rich in fiber, adequate hydration, regular physical activity and a toilet routine.

dose_text: 3.75 mg (1/4 of tab of 15 mg) PO OD
administration: • Place tablet ON tongue and allow to disintegrate until pellets can be swallowed. OR • For the 30 mg tablet, put the tablet or part of a tablet in an oral syringe of 10 mL by withdrawing the plunger, draw up 10 mL of water then shake gently. Administer the dose and rinse with 5 mL of water. • For the 15 mg tablet, put the tablet or part of a tablet in an oral syringe of 5 mL by withdrawing the plunger, draw up 2 mL of water then shake gently. Administer the dose and rinse with 2 mL of water.  • Lansoprazole 3 mg/mL oral solution with sodium bicarbonate is physicochemically stable, but destruction of the enteric coating of the granules in the bicarbonate causes up to 90% loss when administered gastrically. Favor the use of fractions of Fast-tab tablets for optimal use. • Split tablets are stable for 7 days at room temperature when stored in a dry place.
particularity: Usual pediatric dose: 1-2 mg/Kg/day PO divided into 1-2 doses

preparation: Once daily
administration: • Other indications for prednisone or prednisolone are usually Bell's palsy, Henoch-Schönlein purpura or nephrotic syndrome. • The palatability (taste) of prednisone and prednisolone is very poor and may interfere with compliance; when indicated favor dexamethasone. • There is a compound oral solution of prednisone 5 mg/mL. • If compound oral solution not available, use prednisone tablets or tablet fractions of 1, 5 or 50 mg. • To avoid administration errors secondary to several strengths or fractions of tablets, prescribe the oral solution of commercial PredniSOLONE at 1 mg/mL.
particularity: Nil

administration: • PO treatment of periorbital cellulitis is recommended only in patients:• ≥ 6 months of life • Without signs of systemic toxicity • Immunocompetent • Controlled fever  • Criteria for mild periorbital cellulitis, without orbital involvement:• Mild edema and erythema • absence of significant pain • good eyelid opening • normal, non-painful extraocular movements • normal vision • absence of proptosis and chemosis  • If in doubt of orbital involvement or meningeal complication, consult a specialist and refer for hospitalization. • Requires follow-up 24-36 hours with hospitalization if no improvement • Duration of therapy: 10-14 days
particularity: Keep refrigerated

preparation: TID
administration: • Use sotalol tablets or tablet fractions of 80 mg if compound oral solution not available. • There is a compound oral solution of sotalol 5 mg/mL • Dose up to 180 mg/m2/day; the dose increase adds more class III effect (K+ blockade) to the beta-blocking effect (class II) • 12-lead ECG for QTc measurement (max 500 msec) • Torsade de pointes is significantly less common in pediatrics and is often associated with medications combined with electrolyte disorders. • Adjust in renal insufficiency. • Avoid if LVEF < 30%

route: Administer subcutaneous over 30 sec in the abdominal region
administration: • Administer subcutaneous over 30 seconds to the abdominal area. Inject 5 to 10 cm below the belly button, avoiding scarred or inflamed areas. • Visually inspect the solution for particulate matter and discoloration prior to administration.  • For doses < 30 mg, transfer the exact volume needed from the commercial syringe into a graduated syringe for greater precision.
particularity: • Repeatable every 6 hours if response is inadequate or symptoms recur for a maximum of 3 doses. • Onset of action: 30-60 min.  • Therapies for angioedema should never delay securing the airway.

administration: • Use doxycycline tablets or tablet fractions of 100 mg if compound oral solution not available. • There is a compound oral solution of doxycycline 5 mg/mL • Administer with food to reduce gastric irritation, but avoid dairy products (decreased absorption). • Duration of the treatment:• Isolated erythema migrans without systemic symptoms: 10 days • Systemic involvement excluding meningitis or central nervous system involvement: 14 days • Lyme's arthritis or recurrence: 28 days  • If cardiac involvement (except if 1st AV block with PR < 300 msec) or if central nervous system involvement, use ceftriaxone.

dose_text: 125 mg (1/4 of tab of 500 mg) PO TID

preparation: Administer IV slowly over 1 hour
administration: • Use local protocols for repletion with concentrated KCl if available. • Premixed commercial bag at 10 mmol/100 mL (0.1 mmol/mL) • Administer under cardiac monitoring if dose > 0.5 mmol/Kg (max 20 mmol) or speed of administration > 0.5 mmol/Kg/h (max 20 mmol/h) • Reduce rate or administer in the same IV access than maintenance in case of irritation at the infusion site.  • Check serum potassium 1-2 hours after the bolus.
particularity: • Y-site compatible with D5%, NaCl, LR and MgSO4. • 1 mmol KCl = 1 mEq KCl  • Severe hypokalemia is usually defined as a serum potassium < 3 mmol/L or < 3.5 mmol/L with symptoms or ECG changes • Precaution in renal failure; consider the lower dose of 0.5 mmol/Kg (max 10 mmol). • In severe hypokalemia, intracellular potassium deficit can be up to 40-80 mmol/Kg. • Consider concomitant PO administration as higher doses can be given by the PO route.  • Consider serum magnesium measurement and/or magnesium repletion.

preparation: Administer by nebulizer diluted up to 2-5 mL of NaCl 0.9% (Caripul®) or SPECIAL diluent for Flolan®
administration: • Study demonstrates ineffectiveness if administered with standard (jet) nebulizer vs mesh nebulizer. • In a pulmonary hypertension crisis in patients intubated before cardiac arrest, milrinone sould be use over epoprostenol in those situation because of its effect on the right ventricule. Milrinone can be administered directly into the endotracheal tube followed by a flush. (Expert opinion)  • Administrer at a minimal O2 flow rate of 1 L/min • Deposition with repeated use secondary to the glycine contained in the product; check and change the filter more frequently. • The pediatric dose of 1 mcg/Kg q4h is the dose used in practice, despite the paucity of data in the literature, because administration by continuous nebulization causes too much glycine deposits and risks obstructing the filter or the endotracheal tube. This dose, on the other hand, corresponds to a low to moderate dose in continuous nebulization and administration q4h is demonstrated in adults. (Expert opinion)  Reconstitution: Presentation: Vial of Epoprostenol 0.5 mg 1) Add 10 mL of special diluent for Flolan® or 10 mL of sterile water or NaCl 0.9% for Caripul®  2) Concentration after reconstitution = 50 mcg/mL  Presentation: Vial of Epoprostenol 1.5 mg  1) Add 10 mL of special diluent for Flolan® or 10 mL of sterile water or NaCl 0.9% for Caripul®  2) Concentration after reconstitution = 150 mcg/mL
particularity: • NO remains the treatment of choice for pulmonary hypertension in pediatrics; prioritize this treatment if available. • Avoid if pulmonary bleed • Despite significant systemic absorption, inhaled epoprostenol causes less vasodilator effect than continuous IV infusion, according to studies combining pharmacokinetics and cardiac ultrasound measurements. • Inhaled epoprostenol may be combined with inhaled milrinone treatment; alternating administration every 2 hours is recommended in this situation.

route: Add to 25 mL of NaCl 0.9% then start IV at 1.5 mL/h
administration: • Monitor closely for allergic reaction; delayed allergic reaction or serum sickness can occur 10 to 21 days after the administration. • A history of allergic reaction to equine proteins does not constitute a contraindication; consider administration of pre-medication and have epinephrine 1 mg/mL at the bedside. • Do not shake • It is recommended to take samples for toxin detection before giving treatment, but do not wait for confirmation of toxins to begin treatment.

preparation: Administer IV diluted after reconstitution with a 18 micron filter
administration: Additional information:  • If syringe pump not available, administer with low volume tubing or piggyback the entire dose + NaCl 0.9% into a preexisting line containing NaCl 0.9%.   • Treatment of infantile botulism (< 1 year) caused by toxin type A or B • Medication only available through Health Canada's Special Access Program through the California Department of Health Services' Infant Botulism Treatment and Prevention Program (510-231-7600). In Canada, immunoglobulins must be managed by the Blood Bank; contact your local Blood Bank for authorization and ordering of the product. • It is recommended to take samples for toxin detection before giving treatment, but do not wait for confirmation of toxins to begin treatment.    Reconstitution: Presentation: Vial of Botulism immunoglobulin 100 mg 1) Add 2 mL of sterile water 2) Mix gently, roll between hands until completely dissolved to avoid bubble formation. DO NOT shake the bottle.  3) Let dissolve for around 30 min.  4) Concentration after reconstitution = 50 mg/mL 5) Further dilute with NaCl 0.9% 6) Start administration within 2 hours after the reconstitution 7) Rinse tubing with NaCl 0.9% after administration at same rate
particularity: • Keep refrigerated. Stable 4 hours after reconstitution • Y-site compatible with NaCl. No compatibility data with other solutions or other medications. • Contains 1% of human albumin • Risk of allergic or infusion reaction during administration, renal failure (urea/creatinine pre-administration) and aseptic meningitis (like all immunoglobulins, neurological clinical monitoring).

Reconstitution: Presentation: Vial of Botulism immunoglobulin 100 mg 1) Add 2 mL of sterile water 2) Mix gently, roll between hands until completely dissolved to avoid bubble formation. DO NOT shake the bottle.  3) Let dissolve for around 30 min.  4) Concentration after reconstitution = 50 mg/mL 5) Further dilute with NaCl 0.9% 6) Start administration within 2 hours after the reconstitution 7) Rinse tubing with NaCl 0.9% after administration at same rate
particularity: • Keep refrigerated. Stable 4 hours after reconstitution • Y-site compatible with NaCl. No compatibility data with other solutions or other medications. • Contains 1% of human albumin • Risk of allergic or infusion reaction during administration, renal failure (urea/creatinine pre-administration) and aseptic meningitis (like all immunoglobulins, neurological clinical monitoring).

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of NaCl 0,9%
administration: • Administer in combination with sodium thiosulfate as a replacement for hydroxocobalamin in cyanide poisoning. Sodium nitrite and sodium thiosulfate can be administered in the same IV line.  • May induce hypotension. Consider administering over > 5 min if the patient is hemodynamically unstable.  • Measure methemoglobin levels 30–60 minutes post-administration, targeting a level between 20% and 30%. A half-dose of sodium nitrite may be repeated after 30 minutes if methemoglobin levels remain below the target range, after discussion with your poison control center. If methemoglobin >30%, contact the poison control center to validate the use of methylene blue.  • The dose of 6 mg/kg, not adjusted for hemoglobin, is considered safe for patients with hemoglobin >70 g/L. Do not delay administration for hemoglobin results, but contact your poison control center if Hb <70 g/L post-administration.
particularity: • Y-site compatible with sodium thiosulfate. Incompatible with hydroxocobalamin, NaCl, D5%, and LR (no data with NaCl, D5%, and LR). • The antidote of choice for treating cyanide poisoning is hydroxocobalamin. • If combined carbon monoxide poisoning is suspected, contact your poison control center to discuss the risks and benefits.
